# Rename existing `reckoner` plugin id to free the name for the new Reckoner core

## Intent
The plugin id `reckoner` is currently held by the legacy reckoner-apparatus, which the new petition-scheduler design supersedes. Rename the existing plugin id to a non-colliding name so the new Reckoner core can claim `reckoner` cleanly at install time.

## Motivation
The new Reckoner — a petition-scheduler being designed under click `c-mod99ris` — needs the `reckoner` plugin id. The current holder is the soon-to-be-superseded apparatus formerly known as the reckoner. If the rename isn't completed before the new Reckoner core ships, install will collide and the whole new-Reckoner deployment will be blocked.

This is time-sensitive: design work for the new Reckoner is in flight; its install-time slot must be cleared before its core lands.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Pick a name from the `sentinel-` family
Use a name that telegraphs the legacy apparatus's actual role (a standing observer, not a scheduler). This brief does not lock in the exact name — choose one consistent with apparatus-naming convention and document the choice in the rename PR.

### No import compatibility shim
There are no `from '@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus'` imports anywhere in the workspace today (verified during the source observation), so the rename does not need a backward-compat re-export. Do the clean rename.

### Bundle the related doc cleanups
A separate observation flagged that `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md` retains stale prose (drain-predicate language) that no longer matches the apparatus's behavior. Address that doc cleanup as part of this rename so the renamed apparatus's documentation lands consistent in one pass.

### Behavioral cases the design depends on
- Workspace builds clean after the rename with no dangling imports.
- The legacy plugin still installs and registers under its new name (no functional regression).
- The plugin id `reckoner` is free of any registration in the framework after this lands, ready for the new Reckoner core to claim it.

## Out of scope
- Building the new Reckoner core itself — that's a separate (in-flight) commission line; this brief only clears the way.
- Re-export shims or backward-compatible aliases — none needed (verified).

# Rename existing `reckoner` plugin id to free the name for the new Reckoner core

## Intent
The plugin id `reckoner` is currently held by the legacy reckoner-apparatus, which the new petition-scheduler design supersedes. Rename the existing plugin id to a non-colliding name so the new Reckoner core can claim `reckoner` cleanly at install time.

## Motivation
The new Reckoner — a petition-scheduler being designed under click `c-mod99ris` — needs the `reckoner` plugin id. The current holder is the soon-to-be-superseded apparatus formerly known as the reckoner. If the rename isn't completed before the new Reckoner core ships, install will collide and the whole new-Reckoner deployment will be blocked.

This is time-sensitive: design work for the new Reckoner is in flight; its install-time slot must be cleared before its core lands.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Pick a name from the `sentinel-` family
Use a name that telegraphs the legacy apparatus's actual role (a standing observer, not a scheduler). This brief does not lock in the exact name — choose one consistent with apparatus-naming convention and document the choice in the rename PR.

### No import compatibility shim
There are no `from '@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus'` imports anywhere in the workspace today (verified during the source observation), so the rename does not need a backward-compat re-export. Do the clean rename.

### Bundle the related doc cleanups
A separate observation flagged that `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md` retains stale prose (drain-predicate language) that no longer matches the apparatus's behavior. Address that doc cleanup as part of this rename so the renamed apparatus's documentation lands consistent in one pass.

### Behavioral cases the design depends on
- Workspace builds clean after the rename with no dangling imports.
- The legacy plugin still installs and registers under its new name (no functional regression).
- The plugin id `reckoner` is free of any registration in the framework after this lands, ready for the new Reckoner core to claim it.

## Out of scope
- Building the new Reckoner core itself — that's a separate (in-flight) commission line; this brief only clears the way.
- Re-export shims or backward-compatible aliases — none needed (verified).
- The plugin-package `description` field convention drift flagged in the same observation set — surface separately if it bites the rename PR.

# Concurrency audit of summon-relay circuit-breaker under the at-most-twice dispatch contract

## Intent
Audit the summon-relay circuit-breaker's pre-increment counter under the new at-most-twice dispatch contract. The contract change introduced the possibility of two concurrent dispatches racing on the same counter; verify (and, if needed, harden) the increment, threshold check, and breaker-trip semantics under realistic concurrency.

## Motivation
The recent `nsg clock run` + daemon coexistence work surfaced that concurrent dispatch can produce duplicate dispatch rows under specific races, and the at-most-twice contract was the chosen fix. The circuit-breaker that gates summon-relay re-attempts uses a pre-increment-then-check pattern; under the new contract two concurrent attempts can both pass the pre-increment and both proceed past the threshold check, defeating the breaker silently. Once a circuit-breaker fails silently, every downstream guarantee that depends on it — bounded retry, anti-thrash, cost containment — is built on sand.

This is an audit-then-fix commission: the analysis comes first, and the fix shape is whatever the audit recommends (atomic compare-and-set, transactional increment, restructured invariant, or "no fix needed and here's why").

## Non-negotiable decisions

### Audit comes before fix
Produce a written analysis of the failure modes — who increments, who reads, what windows exist between them under the at-most-twice contract — before changing code. The analysis lands as a doc artifact in the commission output. The fix (if any) is justified by the analysis.

### The audit covers the full at-most-twice path, not just the breaker
The pre-increment is one window; the read-back of the counter, the trip emission, and any downstream consumer of the trip signal are all in scope. The audit names every read/write site touching the breaker state.

### Cross-correlated logging lands with the audit
While auditing, add a unique dispatcher-instance id to the daemon log line for each dispatch attempt so post-hoc correlation across concurrent at-most-twice attempts is possible. This was a separate observation from the same source (#4) and bundles cleanly with this audit because the log change makes it possible to validate the audit's claims against real traffic.

### Behavioral cases the design depends on
- Single dispatch path: counter increments and threshold check behave as today.
- Two concurrent dispatches racing past the pre-increment: at most one proceeds past the breaker threshold; the other observes the trip and aborts cleanly.
- A concurrent dispatch arriving after the breaker has tripped: observes the trip and aborts cleanly without further increment.
- Breaker reset semantics under concurrent traffic: a reset doesn't lose increments from in-flight concurrent dispatches.

## Out of scope
- Re-litigating the at-most-twice contract itself — that decision is locked.
- Phase-2 CDC observer racing — that's exactly-once today by transaction inclusion. The source observation flagged it for awareness but explicitly noted it's not the same problem; it gets its own audit if/when needed.
- The CI-noise rewording observation from the same source — that's a parallel doc cleanup, not part of this audit.

## References
- Observation-set w-moevqlgp-3c1d60145c5d (concurrent `nsg clock run` + daemon dispatch races, observations #1 and #4)

1	import { z } from 'zod';
2	import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
3	import { tool } from '@shardworks/tools-apparatus';
4	import type { ClerkApi } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
5	
6	import type { ClockworksApi } from '../types.ts';
7	import { validateSignal } from '../signal-validator.ts';
8	
9	/**
10	 * `signal` — anima-facing event emission.
11	 *
12	 * Animas call this tool to emit custom events into the Clockworks'
13	 * events book. The tool runs three layers of validation before
14	 * delegating to `ClockworksApi.emit`:
15	 *
16	 *   1. Reject reserved framework namespaces (seven catalogued prefixes).
17	 *   2. Reject writ-lifecycle patterns — `<type>.{ready,completed,stuck,
18	 *      failed}` for every type returned by `ClerkApi.listWritTypes()`.
19	 *   3. Reject names not declared under `guild.json` `clockworks.events`.
20	 *
21	 * The tool does not pre-check payload serializability — that is `emit`'s
22	 * sole responsibility (commission decision D11). Validation rejects
23	 * throw plain descriptive `Error` instances (D7); the CLI and
24	 * tool-server layers already format thrown errors for their surfaces.
25	 *
26	 * The tool is restricted to `callableBy: ['anima']` so the patron-facing
27	 * CLI auto-builder does not auto-register a second `nsg signal` command
28	 * alongside the hand-written one in `packages/framework/cli`.
29	 */
30	export default tool({
31	  name: 'signal',
32	  description: 'Emit a custom event into the Clockworks events book.',
33	  instructions:
34	    'Emits a declared custom event. The event name must be declared in ' +
35	    'guild.json under `clockworks.events` and must not collide with ' +
36	    'reserved framework namespaces or writ-lifecycle patterns. Payload ' +
37	    'must be JSON-serializable; pass omit the payload for an event with ' +
38	    'no data (stored as null).',
39	  // Restrict to anima callers so the CLI auto-builder skips this tool
40	  // (D6). The hand-written `nsg signal` command in the framework CLI
41	  // owns the patron-facing surface.
42	  callableBy: ['anima'],
43	  params: {
44	    name: z.string().describe('Event name — must be declared under clockworks.events.'),
45	    payload: z
46	      .unknown()
47	      .optional()
48	      .describe('JSON-serializable event payload. Omitted → null.'),
49	    emitter: z
50	      .string()
51	      .optional()
52	      .describe('Identity of the caller emitting this event. Defaults to "anima".'),
53	  },
54	  handler: async (params) => {
55	    const g = guild();
56	    const clerk = g.apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk');
57	    const clockworks = g.apparatus<ClockworksApi>('clockworks');
58	    const guildConfig = g.guildConfig();
59	
60	    const declaredEvents = guildConfig.clockworks?.events ?? {};
61	    const writTypes = clerk.listWritTypes().map((t) => t.name);
62	
63	    validateSignal(params.name, declaredEvents, writTypes);
64	
65	    const emitter = params.emitter ?? 'anima';
66	    return clockworks.emit(params.name, params.payload, emitter);
67	  },
68	});
69

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Shared `signal` validator — used by both the anima-facing `signal`
3	 * tool and the hand-written `nsg signal` framework CLI command so both
4	 * surfaces reject the same set of event names with identical messages.
5	 *
6	 * The validator performs three checks, in order:
7	 *
8	 *   1. Reject any name whose prefix matches one of the seven catalogued
9	 *      reserved framework namespaces. Comparison is case-sensitive per
10	 *      commission decision D10.
11	 *   2. Reject any name matching a writ-lifecycle pattern —
12	 *      `<type>.{ready,completed,stuck,failed}` for every type returned
13	 *      by `ClerkApi.listWritTypes()` (D3). Suffix-only matching would
14	 *      over-reject custom events that legitimately end in those
15	 *      suffixes.
16	 *   3. Reject any name not declared under `clockworks.events` in
17	 *      `guild.json`.
18	 *
19	 * The validator throws plain `Error` instances with descriptive
20	 * messages (D7). It does not pre-check payload serializability —
21	 * `ClockworksApi.emit` owns that check (D11).
22	 */
23	
24	/**
25	 * The reserved framework namespaces catalogued in
26	 * `docs/reference/core-api.md`'s `isFrameworkEvent` section. Writ
27	 * lifecycle events deliberately live outside this list; they are
28	 * handled by the separate writ-lifecycle check in
29	 * {@link validateSignal} so custom events can coexist with user-defined
30	 * writ types.
31	 *
32	 * `schedule.` is the synthesized namespace for time-driven standing
33	 * orders — only the daemon's scheduler pass is authorized to emit
34	 * `schedule.fired` events into the events book.
35	 */
36	export const RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES: readonly string[] = Object.freeze([
37	  'anima.',
38	  'commission.',
39	  'tool.',
40	  'migration.',
41	  'guild.',
42	  'standing-order.',
43	  'session.',
44	  'schedule.',
45	]);
46	
47	/**
48	 * The four lifecycle suffixes that the framework owns for every writ
49	 * type. Operators may declare arbitrary writ types, but the Clerk's
50	 * phase machine is the only authorized emitter of these events.
51	 */
52	export const WRIT_LIFECYCLE_SUFFIXES: readonly string[] = Object.freeze([
53	  'ready',
54	  'completed',
55	  'stuck',
56	  'failed',
57	]);
58	
59	/**
60	 * Validate a proposed signal name against the three-layer rule set.
61	 *
62	 * @param name            The proposed event name.
63	 * @param declaredEvents  `guild.json` → `clockworks.events`. The keys
64	 *                        are the declared event names; values are
65	 *                        declaration metadata but only the key set is
66	 *                        consulted here.
67	 * @param writTypes       Every writ type name returned by
68	 *                        `ClerkApi.listWritTypes()`.
69	 * @throws Error with a descriptive message when validation fails.
70	 */
71	export function validateSignal(
72	  name: string,
73	  declaredEvents: Readonly<Record<string, unknown>>,
74	  writTypes: readonly string[],
75	): void {
76	  // Layer 1: reserved framework namespaces (case-sensitive per D10).
77	  for (const prefix of RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES) {
78	    if (name.startsWith(prefix)) {
79	      throw new Error(
80	        `signal: "${name}" is in the reserved framework namespace "${prefix}" ` +
81	          `and cannot be emitted via the signal tool. Reserved namespaces are ` +
82	          `owned by the framework.`,
83	      );
84	    }
85	  }
86	
87	  // Layer 2: writ-lifecycle patterns per declared writ type (D3).
88	  for (const writType of writTypes) {
89	    for (const suffix of WRIT_LIFECYCLE_SUFFIXES) {
90	      if (name === `${writType}.${suffix}`) {
91	        throw new Error(
92	          `signal: "${name}" matches the framework-owned writ-lifecycle ` +
93	            `pattern "<type>.${suffix}" for writ type "${writType}". ` +
94	            `Writ lifecycle events are emitted by the Clerk.`,
95	        );
96	      }
97	    }
98	  }
99	
100	  // Layer 3: must be declared in guild.json `clockworks.events`.
101	  if (!Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(declaredEvents, name)) {
102	    throw new Error(
103	      `signal: "${name}" is not declared in guild.json under ` +
104	        `clockworks.events. Declare the event (and a human-readable ` +
105	        `description) before emitting it.`,
106	    );
107	  }
108	}
109

lastPrompt: let's abandon this for now and turn to clockworks. did the apparatus get features for emitting arbitrary event signals or just cron ones?

content.text:
Yes — full arbitrary event emission, not just cron. The cron path was always one of two emission paths; both are now built out and live.

**Two surfaces, one validator:**

- **Anima-facing tool** — `signal` (`callableBy: ['anima']`) at `packages/plugins/clockworks/src/tools/signal.ts`. Wraps `ClockworksApi.emit`.
- **Patron-facing CLI** — `nsg signal <name> [--payload <json>]` at `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/signal.ts`. Hand-written so `<name>` can be positional and `--payload` can be JSON-parsed locally; defaults the emitter to `'operator'`.

Both run identical three-layer validation (`signal-validator.ts`, mirrored inline in the CLI to keep the CLI plugin-package-free):

1. **Reserved framework prefixes** — case-sensitive, currently 8: `anima.`, `commission.`, `tool.`, `migration.`, `guild.`, `standing-order.`, `session.`, `schedule.`
2. **Writ-lifecycle patterns** — rejects `<type>.{ready,completed,stuck,failed}` for every type returned by `ClerkApi.listWritTypes()` (so custom writ types are auto-protected too).
3. **Must be declared** — name must appear as a key under `clockworks.events` in `guild.json`.

**Payload:** anything JSON-serializable. `ClockworksApi.emit` owns the serializability check (commission D11), the validators don't pre-check.

**Cron coexistence:** `schedule.` is in the reserved list precisely so only the daemon's scheduler pass is authorized to emit `schedule.fired` events. Time-driven and signal-driven events ride the same events book; standing orders bind on either.

One side-finding worth flagging since you're in here: the reserved list confirms exactly the gap from earlier — **`book.` and `reckoning.` aren't on it**, so the namespace-spoofing observation from before is real and visible against live code.

What's the angle on Clockworks tonight?

380	          }
381	          schedule.push({
382	            orderIndex: index,
383	            order,
384	            parsed: parsed.parsed,
385	            nextFireTime: computeNextFireTime(parsed.parsed, startTime),
386	          });
387	        }
388	
389	        // ── CDC auto-wiring ───────────────────────────────────────────
390	        //
391	        // Make every plugin-declared book observable as a Clockworks
392	        // event automatically: register a Phase-2 (post-commit) Stacks
393	        // CDC watcher on each declared book that re-emits each
394	        // create/update/delete row mutation as a
395	        // `book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.<verb>` event with emitter
396	        // `'framework'`.
397	        //
398	        // Lifecycle: registration MUST happen here in start(), because
399	        // the Stacks CDC registry seals at `phase:started`; no later
400	        // registration is possible.
401	        //
402	        // Carve-out: `clockworks/events` is excluded. This is an
403	        // *architectural boundary* — auto-wiring the events book would
404	        // pollute the framework event stream with `book.clockworks.
405	        // events.created` rows describing the very acts of emission,
406	        // which is feedback noise without a consumer. The Stacks
407	        // substrate now enforces a Phase-2 cross-transaction re-entry
408	        // depth bound (`MAX_PHASE2_REENTRY_DEPTH` in `stacks-core.ts`)
409	        // that would terminate any runaway loop, so this carve-out is
410	        // no longer the load-bearing safety net it once was — the
411	        // substrate is. Future maintainers: do not remove the carve-out
412	        // on the assumption that the substrate now covers it. The two
413	        // serve different purposes: the substrate caps depth; the
414	        // carve-out keeps the events book free of self-feedback in the
415	        // first place. Every other book — including
416	        // `clockworks/event_dispatches` — is auto-wired.
417	        //
418	        // Phase 2 (failOnError: false): the handler runs after the
419	        // triggering transaction commits. Emit-handler errors are
420	        // logged via Stacks' Phase-2 error path and do not roll back
421	        // the primary write — observation is layered on top of the
422	        // substrate, not gating it.
423	        //
424	        // Enumeration mirrors Stacks' own `reconcileSchemas()`: walk
425	        // `ctx.kits('books')`, treat each entry's value as a record
426	        // of book name → BookSchema, skip silently when the value is
427	        // not a non-null object (matching Stacks' guard exactly so
428	        // divergent reactions to the same malformed contribution
429	        // cannot occur).
430	        for (const entry of ctx.kits('books')) {
431	          const books = entry.value;
432	          if (typeof books !== 'object' || books === null) continue;
433	          for (const bookName of Object.keys(books as Record<string, BookSchema>)) {
434	            // Recursion guard — see above.
435	            if (entry.pluginId === 'clockworks' && bookName === 'events') continue;
436	
437	            stacks.watch<BookEntry>(
438	              entry.pluginId,
439	              bookName,
440	              async (event: ChangeEvent<BookEntry>) => {
441	                // Compose the event name from the delivered CDC event
442	                // — equivalent to (entry.pluginId, bookName) at runtime
443	                // but slightly more robust to future Stacks changes
444	                // and matches the architecture-doc reference sketch.
445	                const verb = CDC_VERB_PAST_TENSE[event.type];
446	                const name = `book.${event.ownerId}.${event.book}.${verb}`;
447	                // Pass the CDC event object through unchanged — there
448	                // is no second consumer to earn a normalized shape, so
449	                // passthrough preserves all available context.
450	                //
451	                // No try/catch: Stacks' Phase-2 error path already
452	                // logs `[stacks] Phase 2 handler error (...)`. Wrapping
453	                // would either duplicate or mask that log line.
454	                await api.emit(name, event, 'framework');
455	              },
456	              { failOnError: false },
457	            );
458	          }
459	        }

25	import type { RelayDefinition } from './relay.ts';
26	
27	// ── Config ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
28	
29	/**
30	 * A custom event declaration in `guild.json` under `clockworks.events`.
31	 *
32	 * Framework events (e.g. `writ.stuck`, `session.end`) are declared by
33	 * the plugins that produce them; only operator-defined events need to
34	 * appear here.
35	 */
36	export interface EventDeclaration {
37	  /** Human-readable description of what this event means. */
38	  description?: string;
39	}
40	
41	/**
42	 * A standing order — a registered response to an event or schedule.
43	 *
44	 * A standing order names exactly one trigger (either `on:` for an
45	 * event-driven order or `schedule:` for a time-driven order) and
46	 * exactly one relay to invoke (`run:`), with an optional parameter
47	 * object (`with:`) handed to the relay as `RelayContext.params`.
48	 *
49	 * Per commission decision D1 the TypeScript type leaves both `on:`
50	 * and `schedule:` optional — the canonical-shape XOR rule (exactly
51	 * one of `on:`/`schedule:` must be present) lives in the
52	 * standing-order validator. The same module is the load-time owner
53	 * for ruling out earlier sugar forms (`summon:`, `brief:`,
54	 * flat-spread params), unknown top-level keys, and invalid `with:`
55	 * shapes.
56	 */
57	export interface StandingOrder {
58	  /**
59	   * Event name to subscribe to — exact match against `EventDoc.name`.
60	   * Mutually exclusive with `schedule:`; exactly one must be present.
61	   */
62	  on?: string;
63	  /**
64	   * Time-trigger expression. Either `@every <N><s|m|h>` or a standard
65	   * 5-field unix cron expression. Mutually exclusive with `on:`;
66	   * exactly one must be present. The validator parse-checks the value
67	   * at guild.json load time using the shared schedule parser, so
68	   * malformed expressions fail loud at boot rather than at first fire.
69	   */
70	  schedule?: string;
71	  /** Name of the relay to invoke when the order fires. */
72	  run: string;
73	  /**
74	   * Optional parameter object passed through to the relay handler as
75	   * `RelayContext.params`. Plain object only; null, arrays, and

lastPrompt: do events have to be registered in guild.json, or can plugin kits contribute them? is the auto wiring of CDC events already implemented?

content.text:
Two answers, both sharper than I expected:

## Q1: events declaration — guild.json only, no kit channel

The picture is layered and there's a real gap:

- **`ClockworksApi.emit()` itself does no name validation.** It only checks payload serializability and persists. Any code holding the apparatus reference can emit any name — plugins, framework, anything.
- **`validateSignal` (the gate for `signal` tool + `nsg signal`) requires `guild.json` declaration.** Three layers: reserved-prefix check, writ-lifecycle pattern check, declared-in-guild.json check.
- **Reserved namespaces are the framework's bypass.** Anything starting with one of the eight reserved prefixes is auto-rejected on the *signal* path, but the framework emits those via direct `api.emit()` calls without ever touching the validator.

**What's missing:** there's no `events` kit type. A new plugin like Reckoner that wants to emit `reckoning.*` has only two options today:
1. Get its prefix added to the hardcoded `RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` const (and emit via direct `api.emit()`).
2. Have someone declare each event in `guild.json` `clockworks.events`.

There's no `supportKit.events` channel to declare-and-reserve in one shot. This is real friction for the multi-plugin world we're heading into. (And it sharpens the earlier triage: the right fix isn't just "add `book.` and `reckoning.` to the const," it's probably "make events kit-contributable, with the prefix reservation falling out automatically.")

## Q2: CDC auto-wiring — yes, fully implemented

`clockworks.ts` lines 389–459, in `start()`. The shape:

- Walks `ctx.kits('books')` over every plugin-declared book.
- Registers a Phase-2 Stacks CDC watcher per `(pluginId, bookName)` pair.
- On every create/update/delete the watcher emits `book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.<verb>` with emitter `'framework'`, payload = the raw `ChangeEvent`.
- `failOnError: false` — handler errors land in Stacks' Phase-2 error log, the primary write doesn't roll back.
- **One carve-out:** `clockworks/events` itself is excluded to prevent self-feedback. The comment is explicit that this is still an architectural boundary even now that the Stacks substrate enforces `MAX_PHASE2_REENTRY_DEPTH` — the substrate caps depth, the carve-out keeps the events book free of self-noise in the first place.

## The intersecting gap worth noting

These two answers talk to each other. CDC auto-wiring **actively emits on the `book.` namespace** as a framework-only stream — but `book.` is **not in `RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES`**. So today the only thing preventing an anima from forging a `book.clerk.writs.created` event is the layer-3 declaration check in `validateSignal`. That's defense-in-depth, not a real reservation. If anyone ever declares a `book.*` event in `guild.json` for legitimate reasons, that wall is gone for that name.

So the brief #1 framing from earlier sharpens: it's not "add a prefix to the const because some future plugin might want it," it's "the framework already emits on this namespace and the lack of reservation is a current spoofing vector."

What were you fishing for?

content: Exploring Clockworks event-emission internals — confirmed arbitrary signals work (validated guild.json-declared names) and CDC auto-wiring is fully shipped. Next: you tell me what you were fishing for so we can dig into it. (disable recaps in /config)

well, I want to make sure both of these features exist. surprised to hear CDC forwarding already landed. when clockworks emits, does it donsi by writing to a book? so this means every update to the db results in a second write to the clockworks table? is that tenable?

message.content: well, I want to make sure both of these features exist. surprised to hear CDC forwarding already landed. when clockworks emits, does it donsi by writing to a book? so this means every update to the db results in a second write to the clockworks table? is that tenable?

content.text:
Yes on both, and the tenability question is the right one to ask.

## Yes, emit is a book write

`ClockworksApi.emit` ends with `await events.put(doc)` — a row write to the `clockworks/events` book. Every emission is a durable row, not a transient pubsub signal. The dispatcher then walks `events.processed = false` rows to fire standing orders.

## Yes, that means 2 writes per primary update — actually a bit more

The auto-wiring path:

1. Primary write to `pluginX/bookY` (in-transaction, gates the operation).
2. Phase-2 CDC watcher fires (post-commit, after the originating tx).
3. Watcher calls `api.emit()` → row written to `clockworks/events`.

So every primary write to *any* plugin book produces one `clockworks/events` row. But there's a small amplifier: **`clockworks/event_dispatches` is auto-wired too** (the carve-out is *only* `clockworks/events` itself). So when a standing order fires:

- 1 write to `clockworks/event_dispatches` (the dispatch row).
- 1 write to `clockworks/events` (the CDC observation of the dispatch row → `book.clockworks.event_dispatches.created`).
- That second events row will itself be observed and re-emitted? No — it's the events book itself, which IS carved out. So the chain terminates.

Net effect: **2 writes per primary plugin-book mutation, 3 writes per fired standing order** (dispatch row + CDC-of-dispatch + the original triggering event was already counted). Phase-2 means none of this gates the primary write — primary tx commits first, observers run after.

## Tenability

For our scale, fine. Worth flagging where the edges are.

**Where it's fine:**

- **No latency tax on primary writes.** Phase-2 = post-commit, so the events book is purely a tail. A slow events write can't make a writ transition slower.
- **The substrate has a depth bound now.** `MAX_PHASE2_REENTRY_DEPTH` in `stacks-core.ts` caps cross-transaction reentry, so even if the carve-out were ever violated, the loop is bounded.
- **The carve-out is principled.** `clockworks/events` is the only excluded book and the comment is explicit that it's an architectural boundary, not a safety hack.
- **Volume in our world is small.** Even busy autonomous operation runs at maybe single-digit writs/sec sustained. A 2–3× amplifier on that is comfortably absorbed by SQLite.

**Where the edges are:**

1. **No retention.** Events accumulate forever. `processed: true` marks them as drained but doesn't delete. Eventually the table grows unboundedly — fine for a research guild, less fine for a long-running production system. There's no TTL, ring buffer, or compaction mechanism in what I read.

2. **Write amplification compounds with bulk operations.** A commission that posts and immediately publishes 10 child writs in a batch produces 10 primary writes + 10 events rows. Still small, but the multiplier is real and grows with the size of the bulk.

3. **At-least-once-ish delivery, not exactly-once.** Phase-2 handlers run post-commit; if the process crashes between primary commit and the watcher's `events.put`, the primary mutation persisted but the corresponding `book.*.created` event row didn't. So `clockworks/events` is *almost* a complete log of mutations, but not quite under crash conditions. Probably acceptable for our use, worth knowing.

4. **The events book is the dispatcher's working set.** Because dispatch is keyed off `events.processed`, the table is doing double duty: durable log + work queue. That's a fine pattern but couples retention policy to dispatcher correctness — you can't truncate ahead of the dispatcher's high-water mark without losing pending dispatches.

5. **Observability is by query.** This is actually the *upside* of the design. You can ask "what fired in the last hour?" or "what writ transitions did we observe?" by querying a book, which is exactly the kind of thing transient pubsub can't give you. The 2× write cost is the price of the queryable log.

**My read:** the design is sound for a research / single-tenant guild and the 2× amplification is a deliberate trade for durable observability. The tenability cliff is **retention**, not write volume — once the events table is large enough to slow up dispatcher sweeps, that's when you'd need to act. Worth opening a click on retention strategy now while we're thinking about it; the answer probably ties into the "shared event-log book pattern" observation from earlier (one of the items in the volume tree we just parked).

Want me to capture a click on the retention question?

content.text:
Done. Root reframed toward scalability (the original implementation goal is preserved in `goalHistory`), three subclicks underneath:

- **Write-volume cliffs** — at what sustained rate / burst size does the 2× amplification, dispatcher sweep cost, or SQLite contention start biting.
- **Retention strategy** — TTL / ring buffer / compaction past dispatcher high-water / different storage primitive — without losing pending dispatches.
- **Crash-window lost messages** — primary tx commits, Phase-2 `events.put` doesn't, which observers silently miss work and how the system reconciles.

    "goal": "Claude-code simplification candidate — second-highest per-LOC cost density in the post-Apr-16 dataset ($0.019/LOC across n=2 sessions, treated as suggestive). Per the Apr-25 complexity audit (packages/plugins/claude-code/COMPLEXITY-AUDIT.md), three forces compound: a runBabysitter orchestrator fusing ~10 responsibilities under one try/catch/finally; an MCP/SSE proxy hand-rolled around 4 interleaved sub-machines; and 4 tombstoned commentary blocks preserving retired-feature history. Three ranked refactor candidates identified: A (extract babysitter-runtime toolkit; small/high), B (decompose runBabysitter and dedup source-mode detection; medium/high), C (extract MCP/SSE proxy as own module; large/medium). Mirror to the animator simplification candidate click c-moe0m38e — both are density hotspots and the audits independently identified the 'single orchestrator fusing many concerns' pattern as substrate-level.",
    "conclusion": "Claude-code complexity diagnosis report landed on main (commit d1ae28e, packages/plugins/claude-code/COMPLEXITY-AUDIT.md). Identified 7 hotspots and 3 ranked refactor candidates: A extract babysitter-runtime toolkit (small/high), B decompose runBabysitter and dedup source-mode detection (medium/high), C extract MCP/SSE proxy module (large/medium). Audit also surfaced cross-pattern observations against the parallel animator audit — single-orchestrator-fusion, tombstone-density, and async-I/O-test-mass patterns identified in both packages, candidates for substrate-level findings. Candidate A dispatched as w-moegbwvv-351c0aefec75.",
    "goal": "Predicted-files gate v0 (soft-warn) commission dispatched as w-moecg331-fd7efad8df99 (codex nexus, complexity 2). Adds manifestFilesCount to PlanDoc, emits astrolabe.plan.files-over-threshold Clockworks event when count exceeds configurable threshold (default 15). No halt, no coupling to laboratory — framework records the signal, sanctum-side instrumentation can subscribe to the event for alerting/dashboarding/etc. Sets up the v1/v2 evolution path captured in c-moe1tb5k (richer signals once we have v0 data validating the threshold).",
    "conclusion": "Predicted-files gate v0 (soft-warn) landed via manual recovery — work succeeded through implement+review+revise, but seal failed 3x due to concurrent-seal contention with four other commissions running simultaneously. Patron rebased the draft branch onto current main (no conflicts) and pushed directly as 047a0927 (astrolabe: add predicted-files-touched soft-warn signal at plan-finalize). Implementation matches brief: PlanDoc.manifestFilesCount field, astrolabe.predictedFilesThreshold config (default 15), manifest-files.ts parser, astrolabe.plan.files-over-threshold Clockworks event emitted strictly after the patch lands with [astrolabe] console.warn breadcrumbs. Writ w-moecg331 stays in 'failed' phase since failed is terminal. Concurrent-seal contention pattern is data for the cost-drift sentinel work (c-modzrgiu) — three retries giving up signals a real coordination-layer fragility.",
    "conclusion": "spider.test.ts split landed cleanly (commit d5b4c06). 10,691-line file relocated into 8 per-feature test files plus a shared spider-test-fixture.ts module. All tests pass; behavior preserved verbatim. Future spider-touching commissions now pay reduced read overhead since most don't need the full test surface. Parent click c-moe1ym8p remains live to track the broader test-fixture consolidation pattern (7 of 10 highest cross-package-coupled files are still tests; this commission addressed only the largest).",
    "conclusion": "String-match mismatch in decision-review's confirm validator. Patron-anima emitted verdicts on all 24 decisions including D17 and D24, both confirm. But the prompt renders 'Primer recommendation' as a short label for long option keys (D17: 'Top-of-handler validation' vs the full option-key sentence) and with markdown backtick formatting (D24: backticks in prompt, plain in emission). Patron-anima followed its instruction to emit the full option key; validator compared against the short/formatted primer-rec string → no match → treated as untouched → escalated. Other low-conf confirms (D5/D8/D9/etc.) passed because their option-key and primer-rec strings were char-identical. Fix: either normalize on the validator (strip formatting, allow prefix match) or normalize on the prompt (always render primer-rec as full option key). Evidence: transcript jsonl 61daa07a in draft-modvzwcl-36d6ac10 shows patron-anima's full emission array; rig-modvzvb3 yields show touchedDecisionIds skipping exactly D17 and D24.",
    "goal": "Commit-time complexity ratchet — quality check at the review/seal stage that rejects agent commits which push touched files past cost-driving complexity thresholds. Distinct from the planning gate (c-moe0l7bl) which prevents expensive commissions from running; this prevents expensive-making changes from landing. Together they bound both ends — what work dispatches, what state the codebase ends up in. Design: ratchet rather than absolute thresholds (only allow changes in the improving direction; legacy files frozen at current state, no further debt accumulated, no blocked work to shrink them). Threshold candidates from Apr-25 per-file data (n=171 touched files): file_LOC > 2500, imports > 12, cross_pkg_lines > 8, cross_pkg_symbols > 13 (set at p95 of observed distribution). Implementation home: review engine (after implement commits, before seal). Computes per-file metrics on diffed files, fails review with structured feedback if any threshold is tripped without ratchet improvement. Open questions: should grandfathered legacy files be visible (a per-file 'high-water mark' shown alongside the diff)? Should the check produce a structured 'budget exceeded' artifact vs unstructured review prose? Should it be soft (warning in review) or hard (review fail)? Likely soft-first to validate thresholds, then hard.",
    "goal": "Animator simplification candidate — top per-LOC cost density in the post-Apr-16 dataset. 4 sessions touching animator (3 of them animator-focused) averaged $13.39 attributed cost per session and $0.018/LOC, vs ~$0.010/LOC average across spider/clerk/astrolabe and $0.005-0.006 in the cheap-package cohort. Three focused-on-animator sessions averaged $28.43 each — most expensive package-focused work in the dataset. Diagnosis pending: likely candidates are the session lifecycle state machine (multiple status transitions, exit-code tracking, heartbeat plumbing), subprocess management (cancelHandle, pgid tracking), and transcript I/O (CDC event chains feeding laboratory). The investment to identify the specific complexity sources is small (read animator/src + recent commit history); payoff if we hit the average per-LOC rate would be roughly halving animator-touching session cost.",
    "goal": "Standing cost-drift sentinel — automated monitor that watches implement-engine cost (and related metrics: turn count, output tokens, cache_read tokens, spec_chars) over time and alerts the patron, or auto-dispatches a remediation, when notable drift is detected. The Apr-16 step-change (avg implement cost 4.80 USD → 8.08 USD day-over-day, sustained) took ~9 days to surface and required ad-hoc sanctum analysis to characterize. A sentinel pattern would catch step-changes within hours: rolling baseline, threshold or change-point detector, alert via patron-facing channel (Oculus pulse, click creation, ledger entry). Open: vigil (scheduled poll) vs sentinel (continuous CDC subscriber on session.completed events) vs reckoner-mediated (treat as a petition the Reckoner schedules). Open: alert-only first vs auto-remediation later. Distinct from the in-flight Overseer (c-moaj06ty) which watches commissions for quality drift; this one watches cost-curve health. Likely shares substrate (event triggers, baseline storage).",
        "goal": "Standing cost-drift sentinel — automated monitor that watches implement-engine cost (and related metrics: turn count, output tokens, cache_read tokens, spec_chars) over time and alerts the patron, or auto-dispatches a remediation, when notable drift is detected. The Apr-16 step-change (.80 → .08 day-over-day, sustained) took ~9 days to surface and required ad-hoc sanctum analysis to characterize. A sentinel pattern would catch step-changes within hours: rolling baseline, threshold or change-point detector, alert via patron-facing channel (Oculus pulse, click creation, ledger entry). Open: vigil (scheduled poll) vs sentinel (continuous CDC subscriber on session.completed events) vs reckoner-mediated (treat as a petition the Reckoner schedules). Open: alert-only first vs auto-remediation later. Distinct from the in-flight Overseer (c-moaj06ty) which watches commissions for quality drift; this one watches cost-curve health. Likely shares substrate (event triggers, baseline storage).",
    "goal": "Cheap-per-task, expensive-at-end verify — restrict per-task <verify> to fast/narrow checks (tsc --noEmit, focused grep, single targeted unit test) and run the full test suite once at session end. Preserves the per-task feedback loop and commit-per-task discipline while shrinking the per-cycle tool_result payload that drives cache-read accumulation. More aggressive cost cut than package-scoping alone; lower quality risk than end-only because typecheck still catches obvious breaks before commit.",
    "goal": "Move verify to end-of-session only — preserve task-by-task structure (decomposition + commit-per-task) but run the verify command once at the end of implementation rather than after every task. Keeps verify cheap (in-context fix loop) but loses per-task feedback; risk is cascading failures across tasks 2-5 from a break introduced at task 2.",
    "conclusion": "Two coupled decisions applied across all 12 Clockworks commission briefs. (1) Standing orders have exactly one canonical form: { on, run, with? }. The summon:/brief: sugar variants anticipated in guild-config.ts's StandingOrder union are dropped; summon is a regular relay invoked via 'run: summon-relay' like any other. Sugar can be reintroduced later if real usage shows demand. (2) Relay params live in a dedicated 'with:' field, not flat-spread onto top-level keys. Top-level is reserved for Clockworks-owned metadata (on, run, schedule, plus future additions like id/enabled/description). Nesting under with: prevents any collision between future Clockworks keywords and user-authored relay params. Pattern follows GitHub Actions. Rationale: the flat-spread shape in the original architecture doc would have required a growing reserved-word list; every feature added to Clockworks would eat a keyword a relay could have used. Captured in brief 04 (dispatcher) with the load-bearing rationale inline; applied consistently in briefs 05 (summon), 11 (cron). Architecture doc refresh tracked in brief 12 (w-modf69vg).",
    "conclusion": "Parent/child (writ.parentId) is the primary ladder edge representation; typed link kinds are reserved for non-tree relationships (cross-product dependencies per c-mod53rpa, auxiliary semantic edges). No link kinds registered in v0 — added when concrete use cases arrive. Rationale: clerk.tree() and countDescendantsByPhase already walk parentId for free; existing writ-tree UIs render without modification; Astrolabe's hybrid pattern (parent/child + astrolabe.lifted-from link kind) is the prior-art template. Scope note: this click decides the EDGE MECHANISM only. The number of fixed ladder levels and what nodes exist at each level is a shape question tracked in c-mod53ood. Mandate-attachment rule ('no shortcuts from product to mandate') is a shape concern, cross-referenced to c-mod53ood rather than resolved here. Mandate parenting from petition acceptance (mandate.parentId = outcome / recursion-node / feature) is cross-referenced to the Reckoner subtree under c-mod99ris.",
    "conclusion": "Option 3 (companion book). Plugin owns a typed book per ladder writ type (tentative names: products, capabilities, outcomes), each keyed by writ id — primary key of the doc IS the writ id, matching the Astrolabe PlanDoc precedent. The writ stays generic; typed fields (priority, stage, owner, etc.) live in the companion doc. Plugin exposes a typed API for create/read/update. Vision text lives in writ.body for v0 — no visionRef field needed; can add later non-breakingly if vision text outgrows the writ body. Lifecycle coupling: ProductStage enum includes 'cancelled' (full set: draft | active | sunset | cancelled); typed API transitions both writ.phase and ProductStage together on cancel/sunset. No CDC-delete, no orphan cleanup — the companion doc survives the writ's terminal states and carries domain-level lifecycle in its own stage field. Rejected Option 1 (body blob — not queryable, wrong semantics), Option 2 (status slot — clerk docs explicitly reserve status for side-channel observations, not intrinsic attributes), Option 4 (typed writ payload — framework change with no clear benefit when Option 3 already provides typed queryable data). Caveat: all names in this conclusion (vision, product, capability, outcome, ProductDoc/ProductStage convention) are placeholders — naming finalization tracked in sibling click c-modeor2d.",
    "goal": "Design the Reckonings book — petition evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event (decision, reason, weighed priority, notes). Schema, retention, query patterns, CDC attachment, relationship to petition state (journal vs. materialized view)",
    "conclusion": "Deferred petition carries: defer_reason (enum: priority | queue_depth | time_hold | patron_policy | other), defer_until (optional timestamp, time-based wake-up), defer_signal (optional event pattern, v0 reserved for future event-based wake-up), defer_count (integer), first_deferred_at, last_deferred_at, defer_note (optional freeform prose). Defer reasons narrowed to product-level Reckoner concerns only — resource_cap, budget, dependency, and rate_limit are handled by Spider/Coinmaster/Animator at the writ level after a petition is accepted, not at petition consideration. queue_depth reserved for strategic hold (Reckoner preserving prioritization authority against a Spider that might be weakly priority-aware); rare in practice if Spider becomes priority-aware (c-mocdmjk1). Wake-up logic on each Reckoner tick: check defer_until past, defer_signal matched, or triggered by writ-completion events. Escalation policies (auto-decline after N defers, nudge priority on repeat defer) are Reckoner config, not petition-shape. Petition lifecycle expanded: new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}; deferred → pending via wake-up; withdrawn is terminal and petitioner-initiated. Terminal: accepted, declined, withdrawn. Non-terminal: new, pending, deferred. Design of the Reckonings evaluation log tracked in c-modc7m16.",
    "goal": "Interim mitigation for retry-rig plan-init collision: today's whole-rig auto-retry always spawns a new rig whose plan-init fails with 'Plan \"<writId>\" already exists' because the original rig's PlanDoc is still there. Today (2026-04-24) 4 writs hit this after a daemon issue caused heartbeat failures across in-flight sessions — the originals went stuck, auto-retry fired, and all 4 retry rigs died at plan-init. The structural fix is c-mocdm2o7 (engine-level retry, no whole-rig recreation), which is already dispatched as commission w-mod0gk8l — but until that lands, every retry attempt poisons the writ with a misleading 'already exists' resolution that masks the original failure cause and requires manual sqlite patching to recover. Question: what's the minimum-effort interim fix? Candidates: (a) disable whole-rig auto-retry entirely until c-mocdm2o7 ships; (b) make plan-init idempotent (adopt-existing if plan id matches); (c) teach Spider to detect 'this writ already has an active rig' and skip retry-spawn; (d) just accept the manual-intervention cost since c-mocdm2o7 is close. Also: when the retry rig fails, the writ's status/resolution gets overwritten with the plan-init error — losing the original heartbeat-failure context; any interim should preserve the original failure signal.",
    "conclusion": "Two CDC-attached channels for petitioner feedback: (1) petition state transitions on the petition record itself (stacks CDC emits events on new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}, plus deferred → pending wake-ups); (2) Reckonings — append-only evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event whether or not it produced a state transition. Petitioners opt into their preferred signal model: event-driven (CDC standing-order on events matching their source), polling (query the book directly), or fire-and-forget (no subscription; re-emit when conditions change). Reckoner writes structured reason fields on each transition: on decline, decline_reason (enum: malformed | duplicate | policy_violation | source_banned | other) + optional remediation_hint (e.g., reference to an existing duplicate petition); on defer, deferral metadata per c-modaqnpt; on accept, writ_id and accepted_at. No direct callback mechanism in v0 — no mandatory subscription, all signals flow through the book via CDC. Reckoner's decline surface intentionally narrow: petition validity only (malformed, duplicate, policy violation, source banned). 'This work isn't worth doing on merit' is not a Reckoner decision — that belongs to the patron; starving-by-repeated-defer is the natural answer for work that keeps losing to competing petitions. Reckonings book design tracked in c-modc7m16.",
    "conclusion": "v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — matching Spider's pattern for substrate uniformity (KISS for MVP). Each tick evaluates the full pending set against current capacity, statelessly; same inputs always produce same outputs. Interval is Clockworks config. Patron immediate fast-path (c-mod9a48y) bypasses the tick. Rejected event-driven triggers (petition.created / writ.terminal / defer signals) + debounce-batching as premature mechanism for MVP; revisit once Clockworks supports event patterns and the polling floor shows real shortcomings. Depends on Clockworks MVP timer apparatus (c-mo1mql8a) providing the cron-style tick primitive.",
    "conclusion": "Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:\n\n- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.\n- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.\n- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.\n- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').\n\nTwo-axis verification falls out cleanly: automated rig-level verification at the mandate/piece layer (contract with the rig); guided patron walkthrough at the feature layer (contract with the patron).\n\nNaming: 'piece' chosen over goal/charge/labor after pressure-testing in real usage contexts (informal reference, nesting, CLI verbs, commit messages, conversational use). Piece is naturally divisible, fits the craft register, has no ghost connotations, and reads frictionlessly across all uses. Collision with Spider's existing 'piece' (atomic sequential task inside a mandate) is being resolved by renaming Spider's piece to 'step' — separate mandate writ to be drafted for that work. Final naming for product/feature/piece (and the XxxDoc convention) still tracked as placeholders in c-modeor2d, but 'piece' for the recursive node is settled here.\n\nRejected S1 (rigid 4 levels — too inflexible per patron pushback), S3 (free parent/child below feature — fragments tooling, dilutes the patron-contract boundary), S4 (only product fixed — loses the patron-contract layer), and S2b (features self-nest — erodes the patron-contract boundary by creating ambiguous validation surface).",
    "goal": "Emit a Lattice pulse when an engine exhausts its retry budget (rig terminally fails): the patron should be informed when automatic recovery gives up, not forced to discover the failure by reading dashboards. Proposed trigger type 'reckoner.engine-exhausted' or similar (final name per Reckoner's trigger-namespace conventions). Payload: rig id, writ id, engine id/design, final error, attempt count, history summary. Implementation deferred — this click captures the requirement and links to the engine-retry design (c-mocdm2o7) that produced the need. Delivery substrate is the Lattice as designed in the concluded notifications umbrella (c-mo1z3teo); this is a new trigger type layering on top of that substrate.",
    "goal": "Rate-limit-aware scheduling: detect when the anima provider is rate-limited (token exhaustion), pause dispatch, and auto-resume when the window reopens. Open sub-questions: (a) detection mechanism — reactive (wait for an engine to fail, enter 'resource exhausted' mode, probe periodically) vs proactive (token-usage heartbeat via cheap Haiku prompt, OAuth usage endpoint if one exists, UI-scraping via Playwright, etc.); (b) state scope — per-engine 'stuck with reason=no-tokens' vs Spider-level 'scheduler paused until tokens available' (guild-wide). Vocabulary: Coinmaster owns token tracking (Purse = per-commission budget); Senti

"goal": "Build Stacks CDC events forwarded through Clockworks — auto-wire book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted} events from Stacks CDC into the Clockworks event stream, so standing orders configured in guild.json can route book changes to premade relays or anima sessions declaratively, with no custom handler code required. Direct CDC subscription remains available for plugin-specific handlers; Clockworks-forwarded events serve operator-level declarative wiring.",
    "conclusion": "Reckoner enforces a Kanban-style WIP ceiling on its own outstanding accepted-but-not-completed commitments (computable from writ store directly: count of writs created via petition acceptance whose phase is non-terminal). Reckoner does NOT peek at Spider's runtime queue depth, parallelism caps, or workspace state — those are Spider-internal concerns owned and enforced by Spider. The two are distinct: 'Reckoner WIP ledger' is within-layer (Reckoner-native data); 'Spider queue depth' is across-layer (would create coordination coupling on a value that's stale the instant it's read). Backpressure when Spider is the bottleneck happens through observable signals (rising outstanding count visible in Reckonings book, queue-depth Sentinel pulses via Lattice, accepted-vs-completed throughput divergence), not control loops — patron or future autonomous interventions react to the signals by adjusting WIP ceiling, budget, or priorities. Properties this preserves: predictability (bounded WIP = bounded surprise for the patron), cost control (caps unrealized commitments under autonomous regime), independent testability (Reckoner testable without Spider machinery), free backpressure (stuck Spider naturally throttles new acceptance via filling WIP ceiling). WIP ceiling is a guild-config policy knob the patron tunes; the Reckoner doesn't compute it dynamically. Specific shape (single number vs per-petitioner vs priority-weighted) deferred — see child click.",
    "goal": "Reckoner-apparatus rename completed via manual push of writ w-moera3n9 (commit 1c15a36). Implementer chose @shardworks/sentinel-apparatus over queue-observer-apparatus — name groups with future sentinel patterns (cost-drift sentinel c-modzrgiu, structural-complexity sentinel c-moegda00). Behavior preservation total: runtime plugin id stays 'reckoner', trigger types stay 'reckoner.writ-stuck/writ-failed/queue-drained', internal symbols and source filenames preserved, apparatus doc filename preserved. Only package.json#name + workspace path + README intro moved. Same 42 tests pass, same 2 pre-existing failures (broken clockworks-retry import after that package's retirement, unrelated orphan-child drain assertion) remain — neither rename-induced. Seal failed 3x at the merge step (same concurrent-contention pattern as gate v0); patron rebased onto current main and pushed directly. Writ stays in 'failed' phase since failed is terminal. The 'reckoner' npm name is now free for the upcoming petition-scheduler Reckoner.",
    "conclusion": "Cartograph scaffold landed cleanly (commit f69f1c6, 9 files, +2110 lines). Apparatus at packages/plugins/cartograph/ contributes vision/charge/piece writ types via Clerk's open registry, opens three companion books (visions/charges/pieces) under owner id 'cartograph', exposes a typed CartographApi enforcing parent invariants (vision has no parent; charge.parentId must be a vision; piece.parentId must be a charge or piece). createX methods write writ + companion doc inside one stacks.transaction for atomicity; transitionX methods couple writ.phase and doc.stage. Six-state mandate-clone lifecycle without childrenBehavior cascade. Notable t3 outcome: astrolabe piece-collision check was a no-op — astrolabe registers only 'step' and 'observation-set' (the spider piece→step rename in b553e49 had already cleared the namespace). Vision-keeper.md is a placeholder; agent runtime is a future commission. CLI follows in w-moera46h-272e31c94197 (now unblocked since the spider.follows dependency is satisfied).",
    "goal": "Standing structural-complexity sentinel — sibling to the cost-drift sentinel (c-modzrgiu) but watching code-shape characteristics rather than dollar cost. Periodically (or on commit / on configurable cadence) compute a snapshot of structural metrics and surface notable drift to the patron via the same alert mechanism the cost sentinel will use. Candidate metrics to watch: per-package source LOC and test LOC trends, per-package inbound/outbound cross-package import edge counts (the cross-package coupling audit's regen script pnpm coupling-audit produces this directly), top-N file LOC outliers (catch the next spider.test.ts before it becomes 10,691 LOC), per-file p95 thresholds for imports / cross_pkg_imports / funcs / exports / types (commit-time ratchet violations roll up here as structural-drift warnings), tombstone density (regex search for retired/deprecated/historical comment patterns — both audits identified this as a substrate-level cost driver). Pairs with the cost sentinel: cost drift tells you 'something got more expensive'; structural drift tells you 'this is the shape change responsible'. Likely shares substrate (event triggers, baseline storage, alert routing) with c-modzrgiu. Open: continuous (subscribe to git-commit events / Stacks book changes) vs scheduled poll (daily / per-commit). Open: alert-only vs auto-create-click for follow-up. Open: where this lives — Laboratory feature, sibling apparatus, or a standalone nsg subcommand. Distinct from in-flight Overseer (c-moaj06ty) which watches commission quality drift; distinct from cost sentinel which watches dollar cost; this one watches the codebase shape drift that drives both.",
