The Apr-16 step-change (.80 → .08 day-over-day, sustained) took ~9 days to surface and required ad-hoc sanctum analysis to characterize. A sentinel pattern would catch step-changes within hours: rolling baseline, threshold or change-point detector, alert via patron-facing channel (Oculus pulse, click creation, ledger entry). Open: vigil (scheduled poll) vs sentinel (continuous CDC subscriber on session.completed events) vs reckoner-mediated (treat as a petition the Reckoner schedules). Open: alert-only first vs auto-remediation later. Distinct from the in-flight Overseer (c-moaj06ty) which watches commissions for quality drift; this one watches cost-curve health. Likely shares substrate (event triggers, baseline storage).",
    "goal": "Cheap-per-task, expensive-at-end verify — restrict per-task <verify> to fast/narrow checks (tsc --noEmit, focused grep, single targeted unit test) and run the full test suite once at session end. Preserves the per-task feedback loop and commit-per-task discipline while shrinking the per-cycle tool_result payload that drives cache-read accumulation. More aggressive cost cut than package-scoping alone; lower quality risk than end-only because typecheck still catches obvious breaks before commit.",
    "goal": "Move verify to end-of-session only — preserve task-by-task structure (decomposition + commit-per-task) but run the verify command once at the end of implementation rather than after every task. Keeps verify cheap (in-context fix loop) but loses per-task feedback; risk is cascading failures across tasks 2-5 from a break introduced at task 2.",
    "conclusion": "Two coupled decisions applied across all 12 Clockworks commission briefs. (1) Standing orders have exactly one canonical form: { on, run, with? }. The summon:/brief: sugar variants anticipated in guild-config.ts's StandingOrder union are dropped; summon is a regular relay invoked via 'run: summon-relay' like any other. Sugar can be reintroduced later if real usage shows demand. (2) Relay params live in a dedicated 'with:' field, not flat-spread onto top-level keys. Top-level is reserved for Clockworks-owned metadata (on, run, schedule, plus future additions like id/enabled/description). Nesting under with: prevents any collision between future Clockworks keywords and user-authored relay params. Pattern follows GitHub Actions. Rationale: the flat-spread shape in the original architecture doc would have required a growing reserved-word list; every feature added to Clockworks would eat a keyword a relay could have used. Captured in brief 04 (dispatcher) with the load-bearing rationale inline; applied consistently in briefs 05 (summon), 11 (cron). Architecture doc refresh tracked in brief 12 (w-modf69vg).",
    "conclusion": "Parent/child (writ.parentId) is the primary ladder edge representation; typed link kinds are reserved for non-tree relationships (cross-product dependencies per c-mod53rpa, auxiliary semantic edges). No link kinds registered in v0 — added when concrete use cases arrive. Rationale: clerk.tree() and countDescendantsByPhase already walk parentId for free; existing writ-tree UIs render without modification; Astrolabe's hybrid pattern (parent/child + astrolabe.lifted-from link kind) is the prior-art template. Scope note: this click decides the EDGE MECHANISM only. The number of fixed ladder levels and what nodes exist at each level is a shape question tracked in c-mod53ood. Mandate-attachment rule ('no shortcuts from product to mandate') is a shape concern, cross-referenced to c-mod53ood rather than resolved here. Mandate parenting from petition acceptance (mandate.parentId = outcome / recursion-node / feature) is cross-referenced to the Reckoner subtree under c-mod99ris.",
    "conclusion": "Option 3 (companion book). Plugin owns a typed book per ladder writ type (tentative names: products, capabilities, outcomes), each keyed by writ id — primary key of the doc IS the writ id, matching the Astrolabe PlanDoc precedent. The writ stays generic; typed fields (priority, stage, owner, etc.) live in the companion doc. Plugin exposes a typed API for create/read/update. Vision text lives in writ.body for v0 — no visionRef field needed; can add later non-breakingly if vision text outgrows the writ body. Lifecycle coupling: ProductStage enum includes 'cancelled' (full set: draft | active | sunset | cancelled); typed API transitions both writ.phase and ProductStage together on cancel/sunset. No CDC-delete, no orphan cleanup — the companion doc survives the writ's terminal states and carries domain-level lifecycle in its own stage field. Rejected Option 1 (body blob — not queryable, wrong semantics), Option 2 (status slot — clerk docs explicitly reserve status for side-channel observations, not intrinsic attributes), Option 4 (typed writ payload — framework change with no clear benefit when Option 3 already provides typed queryable data). Caveat: all names in this conclusion (vision, product, capability, outcome, ProductDoc/ProductStage convention) are placeholders — naming finalization tracked in sibling click c-modeor2d.",
    "goal": "Design the Reckonings book — petition evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event (decision, reason, weighed priority, notes). Schema, retention, query patterns, CDC attachment, relationship to petition state (journal vs. materialized view)",
    "conclusion": "Deferred petition carries: defer_reason (enum: priority | queue_depth | time_hold | patron_policy | other), defer_until (optional timestamp, time-based wake-up), defer_signal (optional event pattern, v0 reserved for future event-based wake-up), defer_count (integer), first_deferred_at, last_deferred_at, defer_note (optional freeform prose). Defer reasons narrowed to product-level Reckoner concerns only — resource_cap, budget, dependency, and rate_limit are handled by Spider/Coinmaster/Animator at the writ level after a petition is accepted, not at petition consideration. queue_depth reserved for strategic hold (Reckoner preserving prioritization authority against a Spider that might be weakly priority-aware); rare in practice if Spider becomes priority-aware (c-mocdmjk1). Wake-up logic on each Reckoner tick: check defer_until past, defer_signal matched, or triggered by writ-completion events. Escalation policies (auto-decline after N defers, nudge priority on repeat defer) are Reckoner config, not petition-shape. Petition lifecycle expanded: new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}; deferred → pending via wake-up; withdrawn is terminal and petitioner-initiated. Terminal: accepted, declined, withdrawn. Non-terminal: new, pending, deferred. Design of the Reckonings evaluation log tracked in c-modc7m16.",
    "goal": "Interim mitigation for retry-rig plan-init collision: today's whole-rig auto-retry always spawns a new rig whose plan-init fails with 'Plan \"<writId>\" already exists' because the original rig's PlanDoc is still there. Today (2026-04-24) 4 writs hit this after a daemon issue caused heartbeat failures across in-flight sessions — the originals went stuck, auto-retry fired, and all 4 retry rigs died at plan-init. The structural fix is c-mocdm2o7 (engine-level retry, no whole-rig recreation), which is already dispatched as commission w-mod0gk8l — but until that lands, every retry attempt poisons the writ with a misleading 'already exists' resolution that masks the original failure cause and requires manual sqlite patching to recover. Question: what's the minimum-effort interim fix? Candidates: (a) disable whole-rig auto-retry entirely until c-mocdm2o7 ships; (b) make plan-init idempotent (adopt-existing if plan id matches); (c) teach Spider to detect 'this writ already has an active rig' and skip retry-spawn; (d) just accept the manual-intervention cost since c-mocdm2o7 is close. Also: when the retry rig fails, the writ's status/resolution gets overwritten with the plan-init error — losing the original heartbeat-failure context; any interim should preserve the original failure signal.",
    "conclusion": "Two CDC-attached channels for petitioner feedback: (1) petition state transitions on the petition record itself (stacks CDC emits events on new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}, plus deferred → pending wake-ups); (2) Reckonings — append-only evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event whether or not it produced a state transition. Petitioners opt into their preferred signal model: event-driven (CDC standing-order on events matching their source), polling (query the book directly), or fire-and-forget (no subscription; re-emit when conditions change). Reckoner writes structured reason fields on each transition: on decline, decline_reason (enum: malformed | duplicate | policy_violation | source_banned | other) + optional remediation_hint (e.g., reference to an existing duplicate petition); on defer, deferral metadata per c-modaqnpt; on accept, writ_id and accepted_at. No direct callback mechanism in v0 — no mandatory subscription, all signals flow through the book via CDC. Reckoner's decline surface intentionally narrow: petition validity only (malformed, duplicate, policy violation, source banned). 'This work isn't worth doing on merit' is not a Reckoner decision — that belongs to the patron; starving-by-repeated-defer is the natural answer for work that keeps losing to competing petitions. Reckonings book design tracked in c-modc7m16.",
    "conclusion": "v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — matching Spider's pattern for substrate uniformity (KISS for MVP). Each tick evaluates the full pending set against current capacity, statelessly; same inputs always produce same outputs. Interval is Clockworks config. Patron immediate fast-path (c-mod9a48y) bypasses the tick. Rejected event-driven triggers (petition.created / writ.terminal / defer signals) + debounce-batching as premature mechanism for MVP; revisit once Clockworks supports event patterns and the polling floor shows real shortcomings. Depends on Clockworks MVP timer apparatus (c-mo1mql8a) providing the cron-style tick primitive.",
    "conclusion": "Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:\n\n- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.\n- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.\n- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.\n- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').\n\nTwo-axis verification falls out cleanly: automated rig-level verification at the mandate/piece layer (contract with the rig); guided patron walkthrough at the feature layer (contract with the patron).\n\nNaming: 'piece' chosen over goal/charge/labor after pressure-testing in real usage contexts (informal reference, nesting, CLI verbs, commit messages, conversational use). Piece is naturally divisible, fits the craft register, has no ghost connotations, and reads frictionlessly across all uses. Collision with Spider's existing 'piece' (atomic sequential task inside a mandate) is being resolved by renaming Spider's piece to 'step' — separate mandate writ to be drafted for that work. Final naming for product/feature/piece (and the XxxDoc convention) still tracked as placeholders in c-modeor2d, but 'piece' for the recursive node is settled here.\n\nRejected S1 (rigid 4 levels — too inflexible per patron pushback), S3 (free parent/child below feature — fragments tooling, dilutes the patron-contract boundary), S4 (only product fixed — loses the patron-contract layer), and S2b (features self-nest — erodes the patron-contract boundary by creating ambiguous validation surface).",
    "goal": "Emit a Lattice pulse when an engine exhausts its retry budget (rig terminally fails): the patron should be informed when automatic recovery gives up, not forced to discover the failure by reading dashboards. Proposed trigger type 'reckoner.engine-exhausted' or similar (final name per Reckoner's trigger-namespace conventions). Payload: rig id, writ id, engine id/design, final error, attempt count, history summary. Implementation deferred — this click captures the requirement and links to the engine-retry design (c-mocdm2o7) that produced the need. Delivery substrate is the Lattice as designed in the concluded notifications umbrella (c-mo1z3teo); this is a new trigger type layering on top of that substrate.",
    "goal": "Rate-limit-aware scheduling: detect when the anima provider is rate-limited (token exhaustion), pause dispatch, and auto-resume when the window reopens. Open sub-questions: (a) detection mechanism — reactive (wait for an engine to fail, enter 'resource exhausted' mode, probe periodically) vs proactive (token-usage heartbeat via cheap Haiku prompt, OAuth usage endpoint if one exists, UI-scraping via Playwright, etc.); (b) state scope — per-engine 'stuck with reason=no-tokens' vs Spider-level 'scheduler paused until tokens available' (guild-wide). Vocabulary: Coinmaster owns token tracking (Purse = per-commission budget); Sentinel is the Reckoner's reserved term for a scoped watcher that emits a Pulse through the Lattice when its condition fires — 'Purse balance exhausted' is one of the explicit example Sentinel conditions in the vocabulary tome.",
    "goal": "Do pulses need a patron-acknowledgment state machine, or should they be immutable event records?",
    "conclusion": "Immutable event records. Ack system removed: no ack_state, no acked_at, no ack/dismiss/resolve CLI verbs. Revised pulse fields: id, source, trigger_type (namespaced string), writ_id (nullable), title, summary, link_url (nullable), context (nullable JSON), created_at, delivery_state (pending | delivered | failed). Supersedes the ack-inclusive shape from payload click c-mob1vzjy. Rationale: single source of truth is the writ; 'still live' is derived by joining pulse to writ state, not tracked on a shadow pulse record. Also keeps the Lattice lean for non-patron consumers (agent-to-agent, anima completion reports) that don't want ack semantics. Rejected: shadow-state ack machine (duplicates writ state), dismiss-with-reason (reason belongs on the writ, not the pulse), audit of patron response time (same signal available from writ-unstuck-time).",
    "conclusion": "Source: writ-state CDC. The Reckoner (guild command-and-control apparatus; see also c-moa42rxh vision-keeper and c-moaj06ty overseer) observes writ-state transitions and emits a pulse — a durable record carrying delivery + ack state — into the Lattice (general-purpose guild messaging apparatus), which delivers directly to the patron. Queue-drained derived in-stream on each terminal transition (check remaining runnable work, emit drain pulse if empty). Writ model stays pure — ack lives on the pulse, not the writ. Rejected: writ-level ack flags, Clockworks events as source (ephemeral), rig-level events (blocks on c-mo3ibj94), inline emit (scatters logic), polling (loses precision).",
    "conclusion": "Read-only. Discord push (fire-and-forget, no interaction) + CLI with three verbs: 'nsg pulse list' (recent pulses, default last 24h), 'nsg pulse list --live' (pulses whose referent condition still holds — derived by joining to writ state), 'nsg pulse show <id>' (detail view). No ack / dismiss / resolve — pulses are immutable event records; 'still needs attention' is derived from guild state (the writ), not tracked on a shadow pulse record. See revision click c-mobzl35x for the revised pulse shape. Rejected: ack state machine (shadow state duplicates what the writ already holds), Discord interactive buttons (requires full bot, not just webhook — MVP boundary), auto-ack on show (keeps read and mutate separate — moot now that mutation is gone), ack/dismiss/resolve CLI verbs (no state to mutate).",
    "conclusion": "Pulse fields: id, source (string — emitter: reckoner, coinmaster, anima, ...), trigger_type (namespaced string — writ.stuck, queue.drained, ...), writ_id (nullable — the subject), title (one-line headline), summary (few lines of human context), link_url (nullable), context (nullable JSON blob), created_at, delivery_state (pending | delivered | failed), ack_state (pending | acked | dismissed | resolved), acked_at (nullable). Strings over enums for trigger_type to keep the Lattice extensible to future producers; channels do best-effort rendering with fallback on unknown types. Rejected for MVP: severity (derivable from trigger_type), recipient/target (patron-only in MVP), correlation_id (no intermediate signals since we trigger on terminal states only), expires_at / grouped_from / parent_pulse_id (rate-limit click territory), per-channel delivery tracking (one real push channel — revisit when multi-channel routing matters). Schema details (column types, indexes, JSON storage strategy) deferred to the planning rig.",
    "goal": "What events should trigger an intervention notification?",
    "goal": "Rewrite patron-anima-prompt.md abstention semantics: 'no principle speaks' becomes 'confirm the analyst at low confidence', not abstain. Abstention reserved for irresolvable conflict or broken frame. Draft at .scratch/patron-anima-prompt-abstention-rewrite.md.",
    "conclusion": "Shipped as part of w-moa5hp3l (nexus c653e59). Razor removed from the attended variant only (sage-primer-attended.md); solo variant (sage-primer-solo.md) preserves the razor verbatim for guilds without a configured patron-anima. Engine selects variant at run time from astrolabe.patronRole config via new astrolabe.reader-analyst engine.",
    "goal": "Author the Distiller interview engine's operational prompt for petition-fleshing. Carries mode-specific discipline opposite-signed from decision-fill: (1) commit to confident specifics when asked scope/feature/product questions — fabrication under taste is the job; (2) abstain (no-signal) is a FAILURE mode — reserved for petitions that are genuinely novel or self-contradictory, not a punt on normal product questions; (3) confidence calibration is fabrication-anchoring — well-anchored in existing patterns/data/surfaces = high, novel specifics = medium, unanchored fabrication = low; (4) output shape is natural-language answers with concrete specifics, not option-selection; (5) when product question can be answered by naming a reader/workflow/decision, do that first before enumerating features; (6) prefer extending existing surfaces (dashboard, plan-review, Oculus) over inventing new ones. Open question: does this engine need a separate Nexus grounding codex (books that exist, surfaces that exist, typical workflows) to fabricate well, or can the role file + petition context carry it? Park that sub-question for after first live use.",
    "goal": "Design the PlanDoc structure that carries the brief + petition + session links + (eventual) Open-Questions handler supplements. The Sage input contract (c-mo3qsanr) named the attachments but didn't specify the document's actual shape — sectioning, embedding-vs-linking, how the existing PlanDoc.Decision/recommendation/rationale fields relate to the brief's six sections, whether the brief replaces or augments the current Astrolabe analyst-output structure. Cross-cuts with c-mo4anoev (handler-output integration) and the Astrolabe pipeline at large.",

toolUseResult.stdout:
"goal": "Build Stacks CDC events forwarded through Clockworks — auto-wire book.<owner>.<bookName>.{created,updated,deleted} events from Stacks CDC into the Clockworks event stream, so standing orders configured in guild.json can route book changes to premade relays or anima sessions declaratively, with no custom handler code required. Direct CDC subscription remains available for plugin-specific handlers; Clockworks-forwarded events serve operator-level declarative wiring.",
    "conclusion": "Reckoner enforces a Kanban-style WIP ceiling on its own outstanding accepted-but-not-completed commitments (computable from writ store directly: count of writs created via petition acceptance whose phase is non-terminal). Reckoner does NOT peek at Spider's runtime queue depth, parallelism caps, or workspace state — those are Spider-internal concerns owned and enforced by Spider. The two are distinct: 'Reckoner WIP ledger' is within-layer (Reckoner-native data); 'Spider queue depth' is across-layer (would create coordination coupling on a value that's stale the instant it's read). Backpressure when Spider is the bottleneck happens through observable signals (rising outstanding count visible in Reckonings book, queue-depth Sentinel pulses via Lattice, accepted-vs-completed throughput divergence), not control loops — patron or future autonomous interventions react to the signals by adjusting WIP ceiling, budget, or priorities. Properties this preserves: predictability (bounded WIP = bounded surprise for the patron), cost control (caps unrealized commitments under autonomous regime), independent testability (Reckoner testable without Spider machinery), free backpressure (stuck Spider naturally throttles new acceptance via filling WIP ceiling). WIP ceiling is a guild-config policy knob the patron tunes; the Reckoner doesn't compute it dynamically. Specific shape (single number vs per-petitioner vs priority-weighted) deferred — see child click.",
    "goal": "Reckoner-apparatus rename completed via manual push of writ w-moera3n9 (commit 1c15a36). Implementer chose @shardworks/sentinel-apparatus over queue-observer-apparatus — name groups with future sentinel patterns (cost-drift sentinel c-modzrgiu, structural-complexity sentinel c-moegda00). Behavior preservation total: runtime plugin id stays 'reckoner', trigger types stay 'reckoner.writ-stuck/writ-failed/queue-drained', internal symbols and source filenames preserved, apparatus doc filename preserved. Only package.json#name + workspace path + README intro moved. Same 42 tests pass, same 2 pre-existing failures (broken clockworks-retry import after that package's retirement, unrelated orphan-child drain assertion) remain — neither rename-induced. Seal failed 3x at the merge step (same concurrent-contention pattern as gate v0); patron rebased onto current main and pushed directly. Writ stays in 'failed' phase since failed is terminal. The 'reckoner' npm name is now free for the upcoming petition-scheduler Reckoner.",
    "conclusion": "Cartograph scaffold landed cleanly (commit f69f1c6, 9 files, +2110 lines). Apparatus at packages/plugins/cartograph/ contributes vision/charge/piece writ types via Clerk's open registry, opens three companion books (visions/charges/pieces) under owner id 'cartograph', exposes a typed CartographApi enforcing parent invariants (vision has no parent; charge.parentId must be a vision; piece.parentId must be a charge or piece). createX methods write writ + companion doc inside one stacks.transaction for atomicity; transitionX methods couple writ.phase and doc.stage. Six-state mandate-clone lifecycle without childrenBehavior cascade. Notable t3 outcome: astrolabe piece-collision check was a no-op — astrolabe registers only 'step' and 'observation-set' (the spider piece→step rename in b553e49 had already cleared the namespace). Vision-keeper.md is a placeholder; agent runtime is a future commission. CLI follows in w-moera46h-272e31c94197 (now unblocked since the spider.follows dependency is satisfied).",
    "goal": "Standing structural-complexity sentinel — sibling to the cost-drift sentinel (c-modzrgiu) but watching code-shape characteristics rather than dollar cost. Periodically (or on commit / on configurable cadence) compute a snapshot of structural metrics and surface notable drift to the patron via the same alert mechanism the cost sentinel will use. Candidate metrics to watch: per-package source LOC and test LOC trends, per-package inbound/outbound cross-package import edge counts (the cross-package coupling audit's regen script pnpm coupling-audit produces this directly), top-N file LOC outliers (catch the next spider.test.ts before it becomes 10,691 LOC), per-file p95 thresholds for imports / cross_pkg_imports / funcs / exports / types (commit-time ratchet violations roll up here as structural-drift warnings), tombstone density (regex search for retired/deprecated/historical comment patterns — both audits identified this as a substrate-level cost driver). Pairs with the cost sentinel: cost drift tells you 'something got more expensive'; structural drift tells you 'this is the shape change responsible'. Likely shares substrate (event triggers, baseline storage, alert routing) with c-modzrgiu. Open: continuous (subscribe to git-commit events / Stacks book changes) vs scheduled poll (daily / per-commit). Open: alert-only vs auto-create-click for follow-up. Open: where this lives — Laboratory feature, sibling apparatus, or a standalone nsg subcommand. Distinct from in-flight Overseer (c-moaj06ty) which watches commission quality drift; distinct from cost sentinel which watches dollar cost; this one watches the codebase shape drift that drives both.",

    "conclusion": "Predicted-files gate v0 (soft-warn) landed via manual recovery — work succeeded through implement+review+revise, but seal failed 3x due to concurrent-seal contention with four other commissions running simultaneously. Patron rebased the draft branch onto current main (no conflicts) and pushed directly as 047a0927 (astrolabe: add predicted-files-touched soft-warn signal at plan-finalize). Implementation matches brief: PlanDoc.manifestFilesCount field, astrolabe.predictedFilesThreshold config (default 15), manifest-files.ts parser, astrolabe.plan.files-over-threshold Clockworks event emitted strictly after the patch lands with [astrolabe] console.warn breadcrumbs. Writ w-moecg331 stays in 'failed' phase since failed is terminal. Concurrent-seal contention pattern is data for the cost-drift sentinel work (c-modzrgiu) — three retries giving up signals a real coordination-layer fragility.",
    "conclusion": "spider.test.ts split landed cleanly (commit d5b4c06). 10,691-line file relocated into 8 per-feature test files plus a shared spider-test-fixture.ts module. All tests pass; behavior preserved verbatim. Future spider-touching commissions now pay reduced read overhead since most don't need the full test surface. Parent click c-moe1ym8p remains live to track the broader test-fixture consolidation pattern (7 of 10 highest cross-package-coupled files are still tests; this commission addressed only the largest).",
    "conclusion": "String-match mismatch in decision-review's confirm validator. Patron-anima emitted verdicts on all 24 decisions including D17 and D24, both confirm. But the prompt renders 'Primer recommendation' as a short label for long option keys (D17: 'Top-of-handler validation' vs the full option-key sentence) and with markdown backtick formatting (D24: backticks in prompt, plain in emission). Patron-anima followed its instruction to emit the full option key; validator compared against the short/formatted primer-rec string → no match → treated as untouched → escalated. Other low-conf confirms (D5/D8/D9/etc.) passed because their option-key and primer-rec strings were char-identical. Fix: either normalize on the validator (strip formatting, allow prefix match) or normalize on the prompt (always render primer-rec as full option key). Evidence: transcript jsonl 61daa07a in draft-modvzwcl-36d6ac10 shows patron-anima's full emission array; rig-modvzvb3 yields show touchedDecisionIds skipping exactly D17 and D24.",
    "goal": "Commit-time complexity ratchet — quality check at the review/seal stage that rejects agent commits which push touched files past cost-driving complexity thresholds. Distinct from the planning gate (c-moe0l7bl) which prevents expensive commissions from running; this prevents expensive-making changes from landing. Together they bound both ends — what work dispatches, what state the codebase ends up in. Design: ratchet rather than absolute thresholds (only allow changes in the improving direction; legacy files frozen at current state, no further debt accumulated, no blocked work to shrink them). Threshold candidates from Apr-25 per-file data (n=171 touched files): file_LOC > 2500, imports > 12, cross_pkg_lines > 8, cross_pkg_symbols > 13 (set at p95 of observed distribution). Implementation home: review engine (after implement commits, before seal). Computes per-file metrics on diffed files, fails review with structured feedback if any threshold is tripped without ratchet improvement. Open questions: should grandfathered legacy files be visible (a per-file 'high-water mark' shown alongside the diff)? Should the check produce a structured 'budget exceeded' artifact vs unstructured review prose? Should it be soft (warning in review) or hard (review fail)? Likely soft-first to validate thresholds, then hard.",

Rationale: clerk.tree() and countDescendantsByPhase already walk parentId for free; existing writ-tree UIs render without modification; Astrolabe's hybrid pattern (parent/child + astrolabe.lifted-from link kind) is the prior-art template. Scope note: this click decides the EDGE MECHANISM only. The number of fixed ladder levels and what nodes exist at each level is a shape question tracked in c-mod53ood. Mandate-attachment rule ('no shortcuts from product to mandate') is a shape concern, cross-referenced to c-mod53ood rather than resolved here. Mandate parenting from petition acceptance (mandate.parentId = outcome / recursion-node / feature) is cross-referenced to the Reckoner subtree under c-mod99ris.",
    "conclusion": "Option 3 (companion book). Plugin owns a typed book per ladder writ type (tentative names: products, capabilities, outcomes), each keyed by writ id — primary key of the doc IS the writ id, matching the Astrolabe PlanDoc precedent. The writ stays generic; typed fields (priority, stage, owner, etc.) live in the companion doc. Plugin exposes a typed API for create/read/update. Vision text lives in writ.body for v0 — no visionRef field needed; can add later non-breakingly if vision text outgrows the writ body. Lifecycle coupling: ProductStage enum includes 'cancelled' (full set: draft | active | sunset | cancelled); typed API transitions both writ.phase and ProductStage together on cancel/sunset. No CDC-delete, no orphan cleanup — the companion doc survives the writ's terminal states and carries domain-level lifecycle in its own stage field. Rejected Option 1 (body blob — not queryable, wrong semantics), Option 2 (status slot — clerk docs explicitly reserve status for side-channel observations, not intrinsic attributes), Option 4 (typed writ payload — framework change with no clear benefit when Option 3 already provides typed queryable data). Caveat: all names in this conclusion (vision, product, capability, outcome, ProductDoc/ProductStage convention) are placeholders — naming finalization tracked in sibling click c-modeor2d.",
    "goal": "Design the Reckonings book — petition evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event (decision, reason, weighed priority, notes). Schema, retention, query patterns, CDC attachment, relationship to petition state (journal vs. materialized view)",
    "conclusion": "Deferred petition carries: defer_reason (enum: priority | queue_depth | time_hold | patron_policy | other), defer_until (optional timestamp, time-based wake-up), defer_signal (optional event pattern, v0 reserved for future event-based wake-up), defer_count (integer), first_deferred_at, last_deferred_at, defer_note (optional freeform prose). Defer reasons narrowed to product-level Reckoner concerns only — resource_cap, budget, dependency, and rate_limit are handled by Spider/Coinmaster/Animator at the writ level after a petition is accepted, not at petition consideration. queue_depth reserved for strategic hold (Reckoner preserving prioritization authority against a Spider that might be weakly priority-aware); rare in practice if Spider becomes priority-aware (c-mocdmjk1). Wake-up logic on each Reckoner tick: check defer_until past, defer_signal matched, or triggered by writ-completion events. Escalation policies (auto-decline after N defers, nudge priority on repeat defer) are Reckoner config, not petition-shape. Petition lifecycle expanded: new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}; deferred → pending via wake-up; withdrawn is terminal and petitioner-initiated. Terminal: accepted, declined, withdrawn. Non-terminal: new, pending, deferred. Design of the Reckonings evaluation log tracked in c-modc7m16.",
    "goal": "Interim mitigation for retry-rig plan-init collision: today's whole-rig auto-retry always spawns a new rig whose plan-init fails with 'Plan \"<writId>\" already exists' because the original rig's PlanDoc is still there. Today (2026-04-24) 4 writs hit this after a daemon issue caused heartbeat failures across in-flight sessions — the originals went stuck, auto-retry fired, and all 4 retry rigs died at plan-init. The structural fix is c-mocdm2o7 (engine-level retry, no whole-rig recreation), which is already dispatched as commission w-mod0gk8l — but until that lands, every retry attempt poisons the writ with a misleading 'already exists' resolution that masks the original failure cause and requires manual sqlite patching to recover. Question: what's the minimum-effort interim fix? Candidates: (a) disable whole-rig auto-retry entirely until c-mocdm2o7 ships; (b) make plan-init idempotent (adopt-existing if plan id matches); (c) teach Spider to detect 'this writ already has an active rig' and skip retry-spawn; (d) just accept the manual-intervention cost since c-mocdm2o7 is close. Also: when the retry rig fails, the writ's status/resolution gets overwritten with the plan-init error — losing the original heartbeat-failure context; any interim should preserve the original failure signal.",
    "conclusion": "Two CDC-attached channels for petitioner feedback: (1) petition state transitions on the petition record itself (stacks CDC emits events on new → pending → {accepted | deferred | declined | withdrawn}, plus deferred → pending wake-ups); (2) Reckonings — append-only evaluation log capturing every Reckoner consideration event whether or not it produced a state transition. Petitioners opt into their preferred signal model: event-driven (CDC standing-order on events matching their source), polling (query the book directly), or fire-and-forget (no subscription; re-emit when conditions change). Reckoner writes structured reason fields on each transition: on decline, decline_reason (enum: malformed | duplicate | policy_violation | source_banned | other) + optional remediation_hint (e.g., reference to an existing duplicate petition); on defer, deferral metadata per c-modaqnpt; on accept, writ_id and accepted_at. No direct callback mechanism in v0 — no mandatory subscription, all signals flow through the book via CDC. Reckoner's decline surface intentionally narrow: petition validity only (malformed, duplicate, policy violation, source banned). 'This work isn't worth doing on merit' is not a Reckoner decision — that belongs to the patron; starving-by-repeated-defer is the natural answer for work that keeps losing to competing petitions. Reckonings book design tracked in c-modc7m16.",
    "conclusion": "v0 Reckoner runs on a fixed-interval polling tick — no event-driven triggers — matching Spider's pattern for substrate uniformity (KISS for MVP). Each tick evaluates the full pending set against current capacity, statelessly; same inputs always produce same outputs. Interval is Clockworks config. Patron immediate fast-path (c-mod9a48y) bypasses the tick. Rejected event-driven triggers (petition.created / writ.terminal / defer signals) + debounce-batching as premature mechanism for MVP; revisit once Clockworks supports event patterns and the polling floor shows real shortcomings. Depends on Clockworks MVP timer apparatus (c-mo1mql8a) providing the cron-style tick primitive.",
    "conclusion": "Ladder shape S2a: fixed top + named first decomposition + recursive node + leaf. Concretely:\n\n- **product** (fixed top) — long-lived; vision; priority. Does not nest.\n- **feature** (fixed first decomposition; parent must be product) — patron-contract boundary. Owns acceptance criteria; the unit of patron walkthrough/validation. Does NOT self-nest. If a feature is too big to validate in one walkthrough, split it into two features at the contract layer.\n- **piece** (recursive decomposition node; parent must be feature or piece) — internal organization layer. Self-nests to arbitrary depth. No patron contract — invisible to the acceptance/walkthrough surface. Holds the 'how the guild organizes its work to satisfy the contract' content.\n- **mandate** (existing leaf) — attaches at any depth under feature: parent must be feature or piece, NEVER directly product (preserves 'no shortcuts from product to mandate').\n\nTwo-axis verification falls out cleanly: automated rig-level verification at the mandate/piece layer (contract with the rig); guided patron walkthrough at the feature layer (contract with the patron).\n\nNaming: 'piece' chosen over goal/charge/labor after pressure-testing in real usage contexts (informal reference, nesting, CLI verbs, commit messages, conversational use). Piece is naturally divisible, fits the craft register, has no ghost connotations, and reads frictionlessly across all uses. Collision with Spider's existing 'piece' (atomic sequential task inside a mandate) is being resolved by renaming Spider's piece to 'step' — separate mandate writ to be drafted for that work. Final naming for product/feature/piece (and the XxxDoc convention) still tracked as placeholders in c-modeor2d, but 'piece' for the recursive node is settled here.\n\nRejected S1 (rigid 4 levels — too inflexible per patron pushback), S3 (free parent/child below feature — fragments tooling, dilutes the patron-contract boundary), S4 (only product fixed — loses the patron-contract layer), and S2b (features self-nest — erodes the patron-contract boundary by creating ambiguous validation surface).",
    "goal": "Emit a Lattice pulse when an engine exhausts its retry budget (rig terminally fails): the patron should be informed when automatic recovery gives up, not forced to discover the failure by reading dashboards. Proposed trigger type 'reckoner.engine-exhausted' or similar (final name per Reckoner's trigger-namespace conventions). Payload: rig id, writ id, engine id/design, final error, attempt count, history summary. Implementation deferred — this click captures the requirement and links to the engine-retry design (c-mocdm2o7) that produced the need. Delivery substrate is the Lattice as designed in the concluded notifications umbrella (c-mo1z3teo); this is a new trigger type layering on top of that substrate.",
    "goal": "Rate-limit-aware scheduling: detect when the anima provider is rate-limited (token exhaustion), pause dispatch, and auto-resume when the window reopens. Open sub-questions: (a) detection mechanism — reactive (wait for an engine to fail, enter 'resource exhausted' mode, probe periodically) vs proactive (token-usage heartbeat via cheap Haiku prompt, OAuth usage endpoint if one exists, UI-scraping via Playwright, etc.); (b) state scope — per-engine 'stuck with reason=no-tokens' vs Spider-level 'scheduler paused until tokens available' (guild-wide). Vocabulary: Coinmaster owns token tracking (Purse = per-commission budget); Sentinel is the Reckoner's reserved term for a scoped watcher that emits a Pulse through the Lattice when its condition fires — 'Purse balance exhausted' is one of the explicit example Sentinel conditions in the vocabulary tome.",
    "goal": "Do pulses need a patron-acknowledgment state machine, or should they be immutable event records?",
    "conclusion": "Immutable event records. Ack system removed: no ack_state, no acked_at, no ack/dismiss/resolve CLI verbs. Revised pulse fields: id, source, trigger_type (namespaced string), writ_id (nullable), title, summary, link_url (nullable), context (nullable JSON), created_at, delivery_state (pending | delivered | failed). Supersedes the ack-inclusive shape from payload click c-mob1vzjy. Rationale: single source of truth is the writ; 'still live' is derived by joining pulse to writ state, not tracked on a shadow pulse record. Also keeps the Lattice lean for non-patron consumers (agent-to-agent, anima completion reports) that don't want ack semantics. Rejected: shadow-state ack machine (duplicates writ state), dismiss-with-reason (reason belongs on the writ, not the pulse), audit of patron response time (same signal available from writ-unstuck-time).",
    "conclusion": "Source: writ-state CDC. The Reckoner (guild command-and-control apparatus; see also c-moa42rxh vision-keeper and c-moaj06ty overseer) observes writ-state transitions and emits a pulse — a durable record carrying delivery + ack state — into the Lattice (general-purpose guild messaging apparatus), which delivers directly to the patron. Queue-drained derived in-stream on each terminal transition (check remaining runnable work, emit drain pulse if empty). Writ model stays pure — ack lives on the pulse, not the writ. Rejected: writ-level ack flags, Clockworks events as source (ephemeral), rig-level events (blocks on c-mo3ibj94), inline emit (scatters logic), polling (loses precision).",
    "conclusion": "Read-only. Discord push (fire-and-forget, no interaction) + CLI with three verbs: 'nsg pulse list' (recent pulses, default last 24h), 'nsg pulse list --live' (pulses whose referent condition still holds — derived by joining to writ state), 'nsg pulse show <id>' (detail view). No ack / dismiss / resolve — pulses are immutable event records; 'still needs attention' is derived from guild state (the writ), not tracked on a shadow pulse record. See revision click c-mobzl35x for the revised pulse shape. Rejected: ack state machine (shadow state duplicates what the writ already holds), Discord interactive buttons (requires full bot, not just webhook — MVP boundary), auto-ack on show (keeps read and mutate separate — moot now that mutation is gone), ack/dismiss/resolve CLI verbs (no state to mutate).",
    "conclusion": "Pulse fields: id, source (string — emitter: reckoner, coinmaster, anima, ...), trigger_type (namespaced string — writ.stuck, queue.drained, ...), writ_id (nullable — the subject), title (one-line headline), summary (few lines of human context), link_url (nullable), context (nullable JSON blob), created_at, delivery_state (pending | delivered | failed), ack_state (pending | acked | dismissed | resolved), acked_at (nullable). Strings over enums for trigger_type to keep the Lattice extensible to future producers; channels do best-effort rendering with fallback on unknown types. Rejected for MVP: severity (derivable from trigger_type), recipient/target (patron-only in MVP), correlation_id (no intermediate signals since we trigger on terminal states only), expires_at / grouped_from / parent_pulse_id (rate-limit click territory), per-channel delivery tracking (one real push channel — revisit when multi-channel routing matters). Schema details (column types, indexes, JSON storage strategy) deferred to the planning rig.",
    "goal": "What events should trigger an intervention notification?",
    "goal": "Rewrite patron-anima-prompt.md abstention semantics: 'no principle speaks' becomes 'confirm the analyst at low confidence', not abstain. Abstention reserved for irresolvable conflict or broken frame. Draft at .scratch/patron-anima-prompt-abstention-rewrite.md.",
    "conclusion": "Shipped as part of w-moa5hp3l (nexus c653e59). Razor removed from the attended variant only (sage-primer-attended.md); solo variant (sage-primer-solo.md) preserves the razor verbatim for guilds without a configured patron-anima. Engine selects variant at run time from astrolabe.patronRole config via new astrolabe.reader-analyst engine.",
    "goal": "Author the Distiller interview engine's operational prompt for petition-fleshing. Carries mode-specific discipline opposite-signed from decision-fill: (1) commit to confident specifics when asked scope/feature/product questions — fabrication under taste is the job; (2) abstain (no-signal) is a FAILURE mode — reserved for petitions that are genuinely novel or self-contradictory, not a punt on normal product questions; (3) confidence calibration is fabrication-anchoring — well-anchored in existing patterns/data/surfaces = high, novel specifics = medium, unanchored fabrication = low; (4) output shape is natural-language answers with concrete specifics, not option-selection; (5) when product question can be answered by naming a reader/workflow/decision, do that first before enumerating features; (6) prefer extending existing surfaces (dashboard, plan-review, Oculus) over inventing new ones. Open question: does this engine need a separate Nexus grounding codex (books that exist, surfaces that exist, typical workflows) to fabricate well, or can the role file + petition context carry it? Park that sub-question for after first live use.",
    "goal": "Design the PlanDoc structure that carries the brief + petition + session links + (eventual) Open-Questions handler supplements. The Sage input contract (c-mo3qsanr) named the attachments but didn't specify the document's actual shape — sectioning, embedding-vs-linking, how the existing PlanDoc.Decision/recommendation/rationale fields relate to the brief's six sections, whether the brief replaces or augments the current Astrolabe analyst-output structure. Cross-cuts with c-mo4anoev (handler-output integration) and the Astrolabe pipeline at large.",

content.text:
State of play, broken into the two pieces:

## Reserved namespaces / spoofing protection

**Two draft mandates are already in the queue** — auto-lifted from earlier observation-sets. Both in `new` (draft), neither dispatched:

| Writ | Title |
|---|---|
| `w-moet01j7` | Reserve `book.` namespace in `RESERVED_EVENT_NAMESPACES` |
| `w-moewkq8a` | `reckoning.` namespace is unreserved — closing it requires a validator change |

So the small-patch answer is one publish-and-go commission away (or two, or one merged). They cover exactly the gap I flagged earlier. They're sitting waiting on us.

**The implementation cost per addition is real but small.** The const lives in two places:

- `packages/plugins/clockworks/src/signal-validator.ts` — canonical
- `packages/framework/cli/src/commands/signal.ts` — hand-mirrored (CLI is plugin-package-free by design)

Plus `signal.test.ts` asserts the exact list contents, so any addition is a 3-spot edit (validator, CLI mirror, test). Manageable but a smell — exactly the kind of drift surface that benefits from a kit-contribution model.

## Plugin kit contribution of events

**Nothing.** No work, no clicks, no writs. The current state:

- `Clockworks.supportKit` exposes `books`, `tools`, `relays` — no `events` kit type exists.
- `validateSignal` consults exactly one source: `guild.json` `clockworks.events`. There's no `ctx.kits('events')` walk anywhere.
- The aspirational comment in `types.ts:32-34` — *"Framework events are declared by the plugins that produce them; only operator-defined events need to appear here"* — is a documentation lie. The only mechanism by which plugins "declare" events today is **the reserved-namespace bypass**: emit via direct `api.emit()` under a reserved prefix, and the validator never sees it because plugins don't go through the validator path.

So plugins can emit (any code with the api reference can), but they can't *declare* in any structured way that survives into the validator's allowed set. New plugins claiming a new prefix have to either (a) edit the hardcoded const, or (b) declare each event individually in `guild.json`.

## The shape of the gap

These two questions are really one question, and the right framing is probably:

> **Should `events` be a plugin-contributable kit, with prefix reservation falling out automatically from the contributions?**

If yes, the model is: a plugin's `supportKit.events` declares both individual event names and (optionally) a namespace prefix the plugin owns. The validator merges kit-declared events with `guild.json`-declared events at boot. The reserved-prefix list becomes the union of (hardcoded framework prefixes) + (kit-claimed prefixes). Two birds, one mechanism — and it kills the CLI-mirror drift surface because the CLI can pull from the same merged set at runtime.

The two existing draft mandates would become unnecessary (they'd fall out of the Clockworks plugin claiming `book.` and the Reckoner plugin claiming `reckoning.` via the new kit channel).

## Recommendation

Two paths, and they're not mutually exclusive:

1. **Tactical** — publish the two existing drafts, eat the const-edit cost twice, move on. Low risk, ships protection now.
2. **Structural** — open a click on the kit-events question, design the contribution shape, dispatch one commission that subsumes both drafts. Higher leverage, takes longer.

My read: the structural answer is the right shape but the tactical patches are *cheap insurance* against the spoofing window before the structural work lands. I'd publish at least the `book.` writ now (the framework actively emits there, so the gap is live) and open a click on the kit-events design. The `reckoning.` writ can wait for the structural answer since nothing emits there yet.

Want me to:
- Open a click on "should events be a kit-contribution mechanism?" (root, no parent)
- Publish `w-moet01j7` (book. reservation) immediately?
- Park `w-moewkq8a` (reckoning. reservation) pending the design?

Or some other combination?

content: We're auditing Clockworks for kit-registered events and reserved namespaces; two draft mandates already exist for `book.` and `reckoning.` reservations, and the kit-contribution channel doesn't exist yet. Next: decide whether to publish the drafts now, open a click on the structural kit-events design, or both.

My instinct is the kit-shape question first since it constrains everything downstream, but I can also see arguing for the migration question first because it tells us how much surface the new mechanism actually has to cover.

content.text:
Captured under the scalability umbrella. Now — kit shape.

## What a contribution actually has to express

Three properties any candidate shape must support, dictated by what's being emitted today:

1. **Specific named events.** `guild.initialized`, `schedule.fired`, `standing-order.failed`. Static, knowable at plugin-declare time.
2. **Dynamic name patterns.** CDC auto-wiring emits `book.<ownerId>.<bookName>.{created|updated|deleted}` — the `ownerId` and `bookName` come from the runtime books-kit walk; the set of valid names is unbounded at declare time.
3. **Operator-defined events.** `guild.json` `clockworks.events` keeps working — operators can declare custom signals without owning a plugin.

Any shape that handles only (1) breaks CDC. Any shape that handles only (2) loses validation strength on the common static case. So the answer is necessarily a hybrid.

## Three concrete options

### Option A — names only, prefix derived

```ts
supportKit: {
  events: {
    'schedule.fired': { description: '...' },
    'standing-order.failed': { description: '...' },
    'guild.initialized': { description: '...' },
  }
}
```
- Plugin enumerates every name. Validator checks exact membership.
- Prefix reservation falls out: any prefix with at least one declared event becomes implicitly reserved.
- **Breaks on CDC** — `book.*` is unbounded; can't enumerate.

### Option B — prefix-claim only

```ts
supportKit: {
  events: {
    namespaces: ['schedule.', 'standing-order.', 'guild.', 'book.'],
  }
}
```
- Plugin claims prefixes; can emit any name under them.
- Validator checks prefix match.
- **Loses typo protection** — a typo'd `schedule.firde` passes validation. **No auto-doc** — no list of legitimate event names.

### Option C — hybrid: named declarations + namespace escape hatch

```ts
supportKit: {
  events: {
    declared: {
      'schedule.fired':         { description: 'Scheduled standing order fired.' },
      'standing-order.failed':  { description: 'Standing-order dispatch failed.' },
      'guild.initialized':      { description: 'First boot of this guild.' },
      'migration.applied':      { description: 'Stacks migration applied to a book.' },
    },
    namespaces: {
      'book.': {
        description: 'CDC events for every plugin-declared book.',
        rationale: 'Names are derived dynamically from the runtime books kit walk.',
      },
    }
  }
}
```

- **Two channels**: `declared` for the static known set, `namespaces` for dynamic-prefix escape hatches that the plugin owns wholesale.
- **Validator order**: exact-name match against merged `declared` (from all kits + `guild.json`); then prefix match against merged `namespaces`; then reject.
- **Prefix reservation falls out automatically** — both channels imply reservation against anima/operator emission on those names/prefixes.
- **Auto-doc generator** can enumerate `declared` directly; `namespaces` get a "this plugin owns dynamic events under this prefix" entry with the rationale.
- **CLI mirror dies** — the framework CLI's `nsg signal` validator pulls the merged set from the running guild at runtime, no hand-copied const.

## Trade-offs summarized

| | A: names-only | B: prefixes-only | C: hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Catches typos | yes | no | yes (on declared); no (on dynamic) |
| Handles CDC dynamic emission | **no** | yes | yes |
| Auto-doc generation | trivial | weak | trivial on declared, prose on dynamic |
| Plugin verbosity | high | low | medium |
| Future schema attachment | trivial | awkward | trivial on declared |
| Operator declarations fit naturally | yes | needs special-casing | yes |

## Payload schemas — defer

Three reasons to leave schemas out of v0:

1. Names alone unblock the structural fix; schemas are pure upside, not on the critical path.
2. The choice (zod / JSON Schema / TypeScript-type slot) opens its own design surface that would derail the kit-shape work.
3. Easy to add later — `declared` entries get a `schema?` field non-breakingly when we're ready.

The validator's existing payload-serializability check (`emit()` does a `JSON.stringify` round-trip) stays as the only payload check until then.

## Recommendation

**Option C — hybrid declarations + namespaces escape hatch, names + descriptions only in v0.**

The dynamic-prefix escape hatch is what lets the structural model survive contact with CDC auto-wiring without bending. The escape hatch should be deliberate friction: every namespace claim requires a `rationale` field documenting *why* this can't be enumerated, so it doesn't become the lazy default.

The migration question (which of the 8 hardcoded prefixes are framework-internal vs plugin-owned) becomes tractable under this shape:

- `schedule.`, `standing-order.`, `migration.`, `guild.` → Clockworks' `declared` block with specific event names (tiny — 4-5 events total).
- `book.` → Clockworks' `namespaces` block with rationale "dynamic per books-kit walk."
- `commission.`, `tool.` → Clerk and Tools apparatuses respectively, in their `declared` blocks.
- `session.`, `anima.` → Animator's `declared` block.

So every existing reserved prefix maps onto either `declared` or `namespaces`, no gaps, and the hardcoded const goes away entirely. That's the litmus test that says C is right-shaped.

## Open question worth flagging

The `declared` channel could also subsume the writ-lifecycle pattern (`<type>.{ready|completed|stuck|failed}`), since Clerk knows its registered writ types. That would put the layer-2 validator check inside the same kit-merge mechanism rather than as a separate parallel path. Worth deciding now or punting?

content.text:
Good constraint to work with. Walking each case — and the discipline forces a useful question to surface, see end.

## Choice rule

When the current prefix is *the domain noun the plugin is built around*, **replace**. When it carries an additional semantic level the prefix should preserve, **prepend**.

## Per-prefix mapping

| Current | Semantic owner | Becomes | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| `anima.<x>` | animator | **prepend** → `animator.anima.<x>` | "anima" is meaningful; Animator emits both anima-specific and session-specific events, so collapsing to `animator.<x>` would mash those domains together |
| `session.<x>` | animator | **prepend** → `animator.session.<x>` | same reason — preserves the anima/session split under one plugin |
| `commission.<x>` | clerk | **prepend** → `clerk.commission.<x>` | Clerk emits across multiple writ types; "commission" is one slice (mandate writs). Prepending leaves room for parallel `clerk.writ.<...>`, `clerk.book.<...>` etc. |
| `tool.<x>` | tools | **replace** → `tools.<x>` | "tool" *is* the plugin's domain noun. `tools.installed`, `tools.removed`. (This also subsumes the earlier "rename `tool.installed` → `plugin.installed`" observation — under this discipline whoever installs plugins owns the prefix; if it's a separate installer plugin, that becomes its prefix instead.) |
| `standing-order.<x>` | clockworks | **prepend** → `clockworks.standing-order.<x>` | preserves "standing-order" as one of Clockworks' domains alongside `clockworks.schedule.<x>` |
| `schedule.<x>` | clockworks | **prepend** → `clockworks.schedule.<x>` | parallel structure to standing-order |
| `migration.<x>` | **stacks** (not clockworks) | **prepend** → `stacks.migration.<x>` | semantic ownership shifts — migrations are a Stacks concept; Clockworks emits them only because of boot ordering. The discipline forces this to its proper home. |
| `book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` | **stacks** (not clockworks) | **prepend** → `stacks.book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` | same reasoning — CDC is a Stacks feature, Clockworks just bridges it to the events stream. The plugin-id prefix should reflect semantic origin, not the writer of the row. |
| `guild.<x>` | *(framework-level)* | **special case** → see below | "guild" is the workspace itself; no plugin owns it |

## The `guild.` problem

`guild.initialized` is emitted by Clockworks, but it's not semantically a Clockworks event — it's a framework-level milestone that Clockworks happens to be the first apparatus positioned to observe and announce. Three options:

1. **Move emission home.** Have `@shardworks/nexus-core` (or whatever the framework runtime owns) emit it, prefix becomes `core.initialized` or `framework.guild.initialized`. Cleanest semantically; requires moving the emit site.
2. **Prefix anyway under the emitter.** `clockworks.guild.initialized`. Honest about who actually emits; loses the "this is a framework event" framing.
3. **Treat `framework.` (or `core.`) as the one always-reserved non-plugin prefix.** A small carve-out — every other prefix is plugin-id, this one is the framework itself. Honest about the special case rather than pretending the framework is a plugin.

I'd go with **(1) + (3) together** — move the emit to nexus-core, claim `core.` (or `framework.`) as the single non-plugin reserved prefix declared by the framework runtime. That keeps the discipline clean (every event has an owner; ownership is structural, not coincidental) and accommodates the unavoidable framework-level events (guild boot, plugin lifecycle, etc.) without contorting them under some plugin's namespace.

## Reckoner

- `reckoning.<x>` → **replace** → `reckoner.<x>` (the plugin id will be `reckoner` after the rename clears the old apparatus's claim).

Tidy.

## Writ-lifecycle pattern

The current `<type>.{ready,completed,stuck,failed}` pattern (where `<type>` is the writ type name, e.g., `mandate.stuck`) is the awkward case. Under plugin-id discipline it becomes `clerk.writ.<type>.<phase>` — Clerk owns it (it's the Clerk's lifecycle), `writ` adds the structural level, `<type>.<phase>` carries the dynamic part. So `mandate.stuck` → `clerk.writ.mandate.stuck`, `step.completed` → `clerk.writ.step.completed`.

This naturally folds the layer-2 validator check (the writ-lifecycle pattern check that currently runs separately) into the dynamic-namespace mechanism — Clerk claims `clerk.writ.` as a dynamic namespace with rationale "writ types are an open registry."

## The interesting tension this exposes

The discipline forces a separation we've been muddling: **emitter** (who calls `api.emit`) vs **semantic owner** (whose domain the event is about). Today they're conflated because the prefixes are domain words; under plugin-id discipline they could diverge.

Two possible rules:

- **A) Prefix = semantic owner.** Migrations and CDC events get `stacks.` prefix even though Clockworks emits them. Bridge plugins emit on prefixes they don't own. Cleaner for consumers; requires the emit API to take an explicit owner argument or trust the caller.
- **B) Prefix = emitter.** Whoever calls `api.emit` claims the prefix. Forces emission to live in the semantically-correct plugin (which has secondary virtue: cleans up cross-plugin emission patterns). Simpler invariant; means moving some emit sites.

I lean (B) because it makes the validator's job trivial (the kit-contribution mechanism just records "plugin X owns prefixes Y") and forces architectural cleanups (Stacks should probably emit its own CDC and migration events anyway, and the Clockworks "bridge" is then just a regular CDC subscriber that happens to forward into the events book). But (A) is honest about the bridge pattern and might better serve cases where one plugin really does observe events on another's behalf.

Want me to capture this as a subclick under the kit-shape question, or fold the resolution directly into the kit-shape conclusion?

content.text:
You're right and my framing was wrong-shaped. Events are *messaging*, not *substrate*. Pulling the events book into Stacks would conflate two concerns that should stay distinct — Stacks-the-substrate is about books, transactions, and observable mutations; Clockworks-the-messenger is about how those (and other) signals get named, declared, dispatched, and consumed. The fact that emit happens to write to a book is an implementation detail of how Clockworks persists messages, not a reason to relocate the abstraction.

## The fix is smaller than I made it

Once events stay in Clockworks permanently, the original "semantic ownership" tension dissolves with one rule:

> **Plugin-id-prefix discipline applies to declaration, not emission.**

- **Declaration** — every event name is declared by exactly one plugin via its kit contribution. The plugin id of the declarer determines the prefix. `stacks.book.*` is declared by Stacks because Stacks owns the *concept*; `stacks.migration.applied` likewise.
- **Emission** — any code with a `clockworks.emit()` reference can emit any declared event. The validator only cares "is this name covered by someone's kit declaration?", not "is the runtime caller the same plugin that declared it?".

Under this rule, the existing architecture is already most of the way there. Today Clockworks walks `ctx.kits('books')` in `start()` and registers per-book CDC watchers via `stacks.watch()`; each watcher calls `api.emit()` with a `book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` name. That code path is structurally correct — Clockworks subscribes to Stacks' CDC observations, formats event messages, persists them. The only thing wrong is the *naming*: the prefix should be `stacks.book.*` to reflect semantic ownership, and Stacks needs to be the one declaring it via the kit mechanism.

So nothing has to move. Migrate two things and the cycle question evaporates:

1. **Stacks contributes a `supportKit.events` kit** declaring `stacks.book.*` (namespace) and `stacks.migration.applied` (declared). Pure declaration — no emit-side dependency on Clockworks.
2. **Clockworks' watcher and migration-emit code start naming events with the new prefixes.** Same code, different string.

Stacks has no compile-time dependency on Clockworks. The kit walk happens during *Clockworks'* `start()` (after Stacks has loaded). The events still get emitted by Clockworks, but they're declared by Stacks.

## The remaining smell, and how to deal with it

Clockworks string-concatenating event names that "belong to" Stacks is a small coupling smell — if Stacks restructures its declared event shape, Clockworks' string-concat code in the CDC watcher silently drifts.

Lightweight fix: **Stacks exports a name-builder function** alongside its kit declaration:

```ts
// In Stacks' public API
export function bookEventName(ownerId: string, bookName: string, verb: CdcVerb): string {
  return `stacks.book.${ownerId}.${bookName}.${verb}`;
}
```

Clockworks' watcher calls `stacks.bookEventName(...)` instead of building the string itself. The contract is enforced at the function boundary; if Stacks ever changes the suffix grammar, Clockworks doesn't break — the function does. Same pattern as Clerk exporting writ-type-related helpers.

This is the cleanest version of "declaration owner controls the shape; emission caller stays plugin-id-agnostic."

## What this means for the kit shape conclusion

The kit-shape work we've been doing is unchanged. What does change is **what we promise the kit declaration enforces**:

- Kit declaration = "this event name is part of my plugin's vocabulary, even if I'm not the runtime caller."
- Validator check = "is this name declared somewhere?" — pure name-set membership, not a plugin-identity match.
- Plugin-id-prefix rule = a *naming convention* on declarations, not a runtime authorization.

The function-shape contribution we landed on still works the same way — Stacks would contribute a static map (just two entries: the `stacks.book.*` namespace and `stacks.migration.applied`), no function needed. Stacks doesn't have to walk the books kit because the namespace claim is enough; the validator doesn't care about the suffix grammar, just the prefix.

Want me to lock this into the kit-shape click as the conclusion (with a note about the name-builder convention), and move on to the layering question? Or pressure-test the "any caller can emit any declared event" relaxation first — it's the load-bearing part of this answer and worth scrutinizing.

content.text:
Yes — I was building a clever rule to avoid moving code, and the clever rule is wrong. Coupling declaration to emission is the right invariant. Walking through what it forces:

## The rule, applied

> **Every event is declared and emitted by the same plugin. Plugin id prefix names the emitter. The semantic source of any underlying state lives in the payload, not the prefix.**

Under this rule, the CDC and migration cases — which I'd been contorting into "Stacks declares, Clockworks emits" — collapse to: **Clockworks emits these, full stop, so they're Clockworks events.**

That's not a fudge. It's actually *truer to what's happening*. Clockworks is the one doing the observation work — subscribing to Stacks' CDC, formatting the message, persisting it into the events book. The `ChangeEvent` payload already carries the original `ownerId` / `book` / `type`, so consumers that care about semantic origin can read it from there. The prefix's job is to identify the emitter, not the underlying state.

## What this forces, case by case

| Event today | Emitter | Becomes |
|---|---|---|
| `book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` | Clockworks (CDC bridge) | `clockworks.book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` |
| `migration.applied` | Clockworks (post-migration emit) | `clockworks.migration.applied` |
| `guild.initialized` | Clockworks (first-boot emit) | `clockworks.guild.initialized` (or `clockworks.first-boot`) |
| `schedule.fired` | Clockworks | `clockworks.schedule.fired` |
| `standing-order.failed` | Clockworks | `clockworks.standing-order.failed` |
| `commission.sealed/.completed` | Clerk | `clerk.commission.sealed/.completed` |
| `<type>.{ready,completed,stuck,failed}` | Clerk (writ-lifecycle-observer) | `clerk.writ.<type>.<phase>` |
| `tool.installed/.removed` | tools-apparatus | `tools.installed/.removed` |
| `session.*`, `anima.*` | Animator | `animator.session.*`, `animator.anima.*` |

Stacks emits nothing. Its `supportKit.events` is empty (or absent). Stacks is silent infrastructure — it changes state, other plugins observe via `stacks.watch()` and emit events about what they saw, under their own prefix.

## The properties that fall out

- **Zero cross-plugin emit handles.** No registered-emitter callbacks, no `stacks.registerEmitter()`, no inversion-of-control plumbing. Every plugin emits only what it owns; nobody emits on anyone else's behalf. The dependency-cycle problem that started this thread evaporates because the work it was solving was self-imposed.
- **Validator is trivial.** Merge every plugin's kit declarations; check the name is in the merged set. No "is the runtime caller the same as the declarer" check, because that invariant is enforced at the *call site* (a plugin can only emit names it declared) rather than at the validator. Coupling is at the declaration boundary, not a runtime check.
- **Plugin-id-prefix discipline is uniform.** Same rule everywhere: prefix = emitting plugin's id. No exceptions, no "well, declared by X but emitted by Y" carve-out. Implementers don't have to memorize a clever rule to know what to do.
- **Events become self-contained per plugin.** Auto-doc generation, event-catalog rendering, validator construction — all walk per-plugin contributions independently. No cross-plugin name-builder helpers, no "Stacks exports a function so Clockworks can format Stacks-prefixed events." Every plugin's vocabulary is closed.
- **Two parallel streams coexist naturally.** Plugins that want rich semantic events about their own state emit them under their own prefix (e.g., `astrolabe.plan.finalized`). Clockworks' generic CDC stream (`clockworks.book.*`) runs alongside as a substrate-level observation channel. Subscribers pick whichever level fits their needs.

## What this means for the kit-shape conclusion

Under strict declaration-emission coupling:

- The kit shape stays **`EventDeclarations | (ctx) => EventDeclarations`** as we landed on.
- `declared` and `namespaces` channels both stay (the namespace channel is now used by the *emitter* to claim its own dynamic-suffix space — Clockworks claims `clockworks.book.` because the suffix is dynamic per books-kit walk).
- The validator is exact-name-then-prefix-match against the kit-merged set, no plugin-identity check.
- The kit-events contribution acts as a closed declaration of a plugin's emission vocabulary. A new emit call site that doesn't match the plugin's declarations should be a build/test failure, not a runtime error — meaning the rule "you can only emit what you declared" wants tooling support (a typed emit API where the names are union-typed off the kit declaration, or an audit/lint pass).

The last bit is the only new concern surfaced: **enforcing the declaration-emission coupling at compile time** rather than relying on developer discipline. Worth a subclick on its own — it's the load-bearing mechanism that makes the principle stick. Want me to capture that?

content.text:
## On the bridge question

Two paths. The thing they're really debating is whether Clockworks should both **carry** events and **manufacture** observations from substrate state.

### Option A — Clockworks emits its own CDC observations

Today's behavior, with naming corrected. Clockworks' declared vocabulary becomes:

- `clockworks.schedule.fired`
- `clockworks.standing-order.failed`
- `clockworks.guild.initialized`
- `clockworks.migration.applied`
- `clockworks.book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` (namespace claim)

**Properties:**
- One plugin, no new install. Cheapest delta from today.
- Clockworks' vocabulary mixes *intrinsic* events (about its own dispatch behavior) with *observational* events (translations of other plugins' state changes). Reading the kit declaration, you see Clockworks claiming to know about every other plugin's books — that's surprising for what's nominally an "events plugin."
- No opt-out. Any guild with Clockworks gets the full CDC stream materialized as events; the events book grows by 1× every primary mutation regardless of whether anything subscribes.

### Option B — new lightweight observer plugin between Stacks and Clockworks

A thin third plugin requires both Stacks (for `watch()`) and Clockworks (for `emit()`). Its only job: walk the books kit, register CDC watchers, format and emit observations. ~30 lines plus tests.

Clockworks' declared vocabulary tightens to its own behavior:
- `clockworks.schedule.fired`
- `clockworks.standing-order.failed`

Bridge plugin's declared vocabulary covers the substrate observations:
- `<bridge>.book.<owner>.<book>.<verb>` (namespace claim)
- `<bridge>.migration.applied`
- maybe `<bridge>.guild.first-boot` (the framework-bootstrap signal)

**Properties:**
- Concerns separate cleanly. Clockworks = messaging primitive. Bridge = "I observe substrate state and announce it." Each plugin's declared vocabulary is tight and predictable.
- Optional. Guilds that don't want CDC-as-events skip the install; events book stays lean. Guilds that do want them install one extra plugin (small cost; could ship in the default template).
- Establishes a pattern. If we ever want other observer-translator plugins (HTTP request observer, file system observer, IPC observer, anything that watches a substrate and emits events), each one is its own plugin under the same shape. Right now there's only one such pattern, so this is upfront investment for a future that may or may not arrive.
- Crosses two compile-time deps (Stacks for `watch`, Clockworks for `emit`). Not bad — it's an adapter; adapters connect things.

### Trade-off summary

| | Option A (Clockworks emits) | Option B (bridge plugin) |
|---|---|---|
| Implementation cost | Rename + relocate ownership | Same code, new plugin shell |
| Clockworks vocabulary | Mixed intrinsic + observational | Intrinsic only |
| Opt-in/out granularity | All-or-nothing with Clockworks | CDC stream optional independently |
| Pattern leverage | None | Establishes "observer-translator" plugin shape |
| Risk of premature plugin-fication | None | Real if CDC is the only bridge ever |
| Conceptual clarity for new readers | "Clockworks emits everything" | "Each plugin announces what it observes" |

### My recommendation

**Option B, with the bridge plugin shipped in the framework default install.**

The deciding factor for me: under the strict declaration-emission rule we just locked, the *kit declaration is a contract* about what a plugin emits. Clockworks declaring `clockworks.book.*` reads as "Clockworks is in the business of emitting events about every other plugin's books," which is a surprising business for an events-messaging plugin. Splitting the bridge out makes each plugin's declared vocabulary an honest, readable contract. The cost is one tiny plugin; the gain is conceptual cleanliness in the part of the system most exposed to plugin authors.

Shipping it in the default install means most users never notice it as a separate concern — they get CDC events out of the box — but it's available as an opt-out lever for guilds that want a lean events stream. (Same rationale as having the bridge be a Phase-2 watcher with `failOnError: false` today: graceful degradation, not gating.)

### Naming

Three candidates, pick whichever rings:

- **herald** — announces events to the assembly. Compact, fits the guild register, telegraphs the role precisely (translator + announcer).
- **chronicle** — records and reports observations. Good register but skews toward "log keeper" rather than "live announcer."
- **observatory** — a place from which observations happen. Cleaner technical fit but more abstract than the others.

I lean **herald**. It's the function the plugin performs (taking an observed change and announcing it on the wire), and the existing apparatus names (loom, parlour, sentinel, oculus, ...) are all single nouns with this kind of metaphorical density.

Want to lock this in, or pressure-test the "premature plugin-fication" worry first?
